It uses a separate small passage downstream of the throttle butterfly where suction is still strong enough to draw fuel. Without this system, the engine would starve and stop at idle.
exam_tip: The idling system delivers fuel at low throttle when airflow is too slow for the main jet to work.
key_facts: Provides fuel when the throttle is nearly closed
Uses a separate passage downstream of the throttle butterfly
Takes over from the main jet at low power settings
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor: (e) a power enrichment (economizer) system
concept: The power enrichment (economiser) system automatically richens the mixture at high power settings. At full throttle, extra fuel is needed for maximum power and to cool the cylinders. The economiser opens an additional fuel passage when manifold pressure is high, enriching the mixture beyond the normal metering. At lower power settings, it stays closed for fuel economy.
exam_tip: The economiser richens the mixture at high power for max power and cooling -- closed at cruise for fuel economy.
key_facts: Automatically enriches mixture at high power settings (high MAP)
Extra fuel provides maximum power and cylinder cooling
Closes at lower power settings to maintain fuel economy
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor: (f) a mixture control
concept: The mixture control allows the pilot to manually adjust the fuel-air ratio. As altitude increases, air density decreases but the carburettor still delivers the same fuel flow, making the mixture progressively richer. The pilot leans the mixture to compensate. At low altitudes, the mixture is set to full rich for take-off and climb.
exam_tip: Lean the mixture as you climb to compensate for decreasing air density -- full rich for take-off and landing.
key_facts: Allows manual adjustment of fuel-air ratio
As altitude increases, air thins but fuel flow stays constant -- mixture goes rich
Lean during cruise at altitude; full rich for take-off, climb, and landing
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor: (g) a cut-off system
concept: The cut-off system shuts off fuel flow to the engine completely when the mixture control is pulled to the idle cut-off position. This is the normal way to shut down an aircraft engine -- cutting the fuel rather than switching off the ignition. Shutting down by fuel starvation prevents fuel pooling in the cylinders, which reduces the risk of fire on the next start.
exam_tip: Engines are shut down using mixture cut-off (not ignition off) -- this prevents fuel pooling in the cylinders.
key_facts: Idle cut-off stops all fuel flow to the engine
Normal shutdown procedure: mixture to idle cut-off
Prevents raw fuel accumulating in the induction system
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the disadvantages of a float type carburettor in an aero-engine.
concept: Float carbs struggle with the variable g-forces in aircraft. A hard turn, rough turbulence, or inverted flight can slosh the fuel around in the float chamber, causing the level to rise or fall erratically. The fuel supply to the engine becomes inconsistent. Carburettor ice is another killer—as fuel evaporates in the venturi, the pressure drop cools the fuel. In humid conditions around freezing, ice can form and block the venturi, starving the engine. Modern carb heaters help, but it's still a threat. Float carbs also suffer from poor distribution of fuel across multiple cylinders—some cylinders get richer mixture than others, leading to uneven running. They're altitude-sensitive because the fuel flow's driven by air pressure drop; at higher altitude where air's thinner, the venturi effect weakens and fuel flow reduces (mixture leans) even without you touching anything. They're slow to respond to sudden power changes—that accelerator pump does its best, but lag is noticeable. Fuel injection solved most of these problems, which is why it's now standard on modern aircraft.
exam_tip: Float carbs have: slosh issues in manoeuvres, carb ice risk, poor fuel distribution, altitude sensitivity, slow response. Know why fuel injection's better.
key_facts: G-forces cause fuel slosh and inconsistency
Carburettor ice risk in humid cold
Poor fuel distribution between cylinders
Altitude sensitivity (mixture leans automatically)
Slow throttle response
Why industry moved to fuel injection
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the correct use of the mixture control.
concept: The mixture control adjusts how much fuel flows relative to air, allowing you to maintain proper ratio at different altitudes. At sea level (Ardmore), the air's dense and you typically fly at full rich—plenty of fuel because there's plenty of air. As you climb and air gets thinner, the venturi effect weakens and the mixture leans. If you didn't adjust, you'd end up too lean at altitude. So you lean out the mixture control—reducing fuel flow—to maintain the proper ratio. The rule of thumb: climb to altitude, reduce power, lean gradually by pulling the mixture knob while watching the engine response (rough slightly, then smooth again). At cruise you lean for economy. If you're on final approach and descending into denser air, you should enrich the mixture again or the engine might run rough. Never go full lean in flight except to shut down at the end. Proper leaning extends range, prevents plug fouling, and keeps the engine happy. Improper use (too rich or too lean) wastes fuel or risks detonation.
exam_tip: Altitude changes = mixture adjustment. Climb = lean for altitude. Descend = enrich as you enter denser air. Full rich for takeoff. Never abuse mixture.
key_facts: Adjusts fuel-to-air ratio for altitude
Full rich at takeoff (sea level)
Lean gradually during climb
Proper cruise leaning extends range
Enrich when descending into denser air
Prevents fouling and detonation if used correctly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the consequences of operating with over-rich and over-lean mixture settings.
concept: Over-rich (too much fuel): engine runs rough and black-smoky, power drops, fuel consumption skyrockets, and spark plugs foul with excess carbon and soot. You'll see black smoke from the exhaust. The mixture doesn't burn completely, wasting fuel and building up deposits that eventually foul plugs so badly the engine barely runs. Fuel also dilutes the engine oil (raw fuel running down cylinder walls), thinning it and reducing its protective properties. Over-lean (too little fuel): the mixture burns hotter, risking detonation. Engine runs rough and hot, power drops, and sustained lean running can damage pistons and valves. Some cylinders may run so lean they misfire. The engine sounds stressed, RPM becomes inconsistent. Very lean mixture can cause backfiring (fuel igniting in the intake manifold) or popping on deceleration. Both extremes are bad; you want to maintain proper ratio. A slightly rich mixture is usually safer operationally (though wasteful) than too lean.
exam_tip: Over-rich: black smoke, fouling, wasted fuel, rough. Over-lean: hot, detonation risk, rough, potential damage. Know the dangers of both.
key_facts: Over-rich: rough, black smoke, fouling, wasted fuel
Deposits foul spark plugs
Fuel thins engine oil
Over-lean: hot, detonation risk, rough
Can damage pistons and valves
Lean mixture can cause backfire
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor ice, explain the process and atmospheric conditions for the formation of: (a) refrigeration (or fuel) ice
concept: Refrigeration (fuel) ice forms when fuel evaporates in the carburettor venturi, absorbing heat and dropping the temperature by up to 20-30 degrees C. This can cause ice to form on the carburettor walls and throttle butterfly even when the outside air temperature is well above freezing -- it can occur in OATs up to about 30 degrees C with sufficient humidity.
exam_tip: Fuel evaporation ice can form in OATs up to 30 degrees C -- temperature drops 20-30 degrees C in the venturi.
key_facts: Fuel evaporation absorbs heat, dropping temperature 20-30 degrees C
Can occur in OATs from -10 degrees C to +30 degrees C if humidity is adequate
Most common and dangerous type of carburettor ice
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor ice, explain the process and atmospheric conditions for the formation of: (b) throttle ice
concept: Throttle ice forms on and around the throttle butterfly valve due to the pressure drop as air accelerates past the partially closed throttle. This local cooling effect is similar to what happens when you let air out of a tyre -- the expanding gas cools rapidly. Throttle ice is most likely at low power settings where the butterfly is nearly closed.
exam_tip: Throttle ice forms at low power settings due to pressure drop across the throttle butterfly.
key_facts: Forms due to pressure (adiabatic) cooling at the throttle butterfly
Most likely at low power settings with throttle partially closed
Often combines with fuel ice to worsen the overall icing risk
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor ice, explain the process and atmospheric conditions for the formation of: (c) impact ice
concept: Impact ice forms when visible moisture (rain, drizzle, or cloud) freezes on contact with cold surfaces at the carburettor air intake. It requires the OAT to be at or below freezing and visible moisture to be present. Impact ice can block the air filter and reduce or stop airflow to the engine.
exam_tip: Impact ice needs sub-zero temperatures AND visible moisture -- it blocks the air intake.
key_facts: Requires OAT at or below 0 degrees C and visible moisture
Forms on the air intake filter and ducting
Can be avoided by selecting alternate (unfiltered) air intake
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the: (a) normal symptoms of carburettor ice formation
concept: The classic symptom of carburettor ice in a fixed-pitch aircraft is a gradual drop in RPM with no change in throttle position. In a constant-speed prop aircraft, MAP drops while RPM initially stays constant (the CSU compensates). Other symptoms include rough running and eventual engine stoppage if not addressed.
exam_tip: Fixed pitch: RPM drops. Constant speed: MAP drops first (CSU holds RPM temporarily). Both: rough running if severe.
key_facts: Fixed-pitch aircraft: progressive RPM drop with throttle unchanged
Constant-speed prop: MAP drops, RPM may remain steady initially
If unchecked, leads to rough running, power loss, and possible engine stoppage
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the: (b) the correct use of the carburettor heat control
concept: Apply full carburettor heat at the first sign of ice. Expect a further temporary RPM drop (or rough running) as the hot air melts the ice and the resulting water passes through the engine. RPM will then recover above the original iced-up value. In icing conditions, use carb heat preventively rather than waiting for symptoms. Remove carb heat for take-off (it reduces power and removes air filtering).
exam_tip: Apply full carb heat at first sign of ice -- expect a temporary drop then recovery. Use preventively in icing conditions.
key_facts: Apply full carb heat immediately when ice is suspected
Expect temporary RPM drop as ice melts, then recovery
Remove carb heat for take-off and go-arounds (it reduces power and disables air filter)
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe how carburettor icing can prevent the normal operation of an aeroengine.
concept: Carb ice blocks the flow path—either in the venturi where fuel's drawn, or in the throttle valve itself. If the venturi ices, fuel can't be sucked effectively into the airstream, so the mixture leans dramatically and the engine loses power. If throttle ice forms and freezes the butterfly solid, you can't control power with the throttle—it's stuck at whatever position it was at. Either way, you lose smooth power control and the engine runs rough. In extreme cases, if the ice completely blocks the venturi, airflow stops, and the engine quits entirely. This is why carb ice is a real threat—it doesn't just make the engine run poorly; it can actually prevent operation entirely. You can't diagnose the exact icing location by feel, so your response is always the same: apply carb heat and reduce power to let it melt. If ice is severe, you might need to descend into warmer air and keep carb heat on until it clears. A completely blocked carb means engine failure; that's why prevention (staying out of icing conditions or using carb heat prophylactically) is critical.
exam_tip: Ice blocks flow paths. Venturi ice = fuel starvation. Throttle ice = no power control. Either can cause total engine failure. Prevention critical.
key_facts: Ice blocks venturi or throttle passage
Fuel starvation from venturi ice
Loss of power control from throttle ice
Rough running, power loss, or complete failure
May need descent into warmer air
Prevention: carb heat, avoid icing conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of excessive carburettor heat in high output engines.
concept: Excessive carb heat is hot air drawn from around the exhaust manifold, and in high-power engines (or at high power settings), that air can be extremely hot—potentially 300°C or more. If you leave carb heat on continuously at high power, you're running an abnormally hot mixture. This increases combustion temperature significantly, raising detonation risk. You also lose air density benefit (hot air's less dense, less oxygen per volume), so power actually decreases. Additionally, excessive heat can warp or damage carb components, potentially causing fuel vaporization in the lines (vapor lock) where fuel boils before reaching the carb, interrupting supply. Most engines are designed to use carb heat only when needed for ice prevention, not continuously. The rule: use carb heat only in icing conditions or immediately after landing where carb ice risk is highest. Continuous carb heat on high-power flight is a recipe for detonation problems and potential component damage. Some high-performance engines even have carb heat limiting devices to prevent misuse.
exam_tip: Excessive carb heat in high power = detonation risk, power loss, potential vapor lock. Use carb heat only when needed for ice, not routinely in cruise.
key_facts: Hot air from exhaust can exceed 300°C
Raises combustion temperature
Increases detonation risk at high power
Reduces air density (power loss)
Can cause vapor lock in fuel system
Use only for ice prevention, not routinely
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor air intakes, explain the correct use of: (a) ram air
concept: Ram air is the normal air intake that faces into the slipstream, using the aircraft's forward speed to create a slight ram-air pressure increase at the carburettor inlet. At cruise speeds, this ram effect provides a small boost to manifold pressure. Ram air passes through the air filter, which keeps out dust and debris.
exam_tip: Ram air is the normal filtered intake -- forward speed provides a small pressure boost at the carburettor.
key_facts: Normal air intake facing into the slipstream
Air is filtered to remove contaminants
Forward speed creates a small ram-air pressure increase
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor air intakes, explain the correct use of: (b) filtered air
concept: Filtered air passes through the intake filter before reaching the carburettor. The filter removes dust, insects, and debris that could damage engine components or block jets. Filtered air is the default intake selection for all ground operations and most flight conditions. On dusty strips, a clean filter is particularly important.
exam_tip: Filtered air is the default intake -- the filter protects the engine from dust, insects, and debris.
key_facts: Air filter removes dust, insects, and debris
Default intake selection for ground and most flight operations
A blocked filter restricts airflow and reduces engine performance
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor air intakes, explain the correct use of: (c) carburettor heat
concept: Carburettor heat uses hot air from around the exhaust manifold to warm the intake air and prevent or clear carburettor ice. Applying carb heat bypasses the air filter and reduces air density, so there's a power loss of about 10-15%. Use it to prevent or clear carburettor ice, but remove it for take-off and go-arounds where full power is needed.
exam_tip: Carb heat bypasses the filter and costs 10-15% power -- use it for ice prevention but not for take-off.
key_facts: Hot air sourced from around the exhaust manifold
Bypasses the air filter -- unfiltered air enters the engine
Reduces power by approximately 10-15% due to decreased air density
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the partial blockage of the engine air intake filter.
concept: An air intake filter collects dust, sand, pollen, and other particles to protect the engine. As it fills up with contaminants, the filter material becomes increasingly restricted—partial blockage. This restriction increases intake manifold vacuum (suction) below the throttle, which affects the carburettor's metering jets and creates several problems. The mixture leans (less fuel relative to air) because the metering system senses different pressure conditions. You lose power and the engine runs rough. Fuel consumption might drop momentarily (lighter load), but efficiency drops too. If blockage is severe enough, you might not be able to generate enough airflow to draw fuel properly, and the engine might even stall. On some aircraft, a clogged filter will trigger an annunciator light or indicator. The fix: replace the filter. You should check filters regularly, especially if you're operating in dusty conditions (arid areas around the New Zealand interior). Partial blockage might not cause immediate failure, but it degrades performance. A badly blocked filter will prevent engine operation entirely.
exam_tip: Blockage = pressure drop = intake vacuum increases = mixture leans. Result: rough, power loss, fouling potential. Know that partial blockage is insidious.
key_facts: Partial blockage restricts airflow
Increases intake manifold vacuum
Mixture leans (unexpected)
Power loss and rough running
Fuel consumption effects
Complete blockage can stall engine
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for a reduction in power when carburettor heat is operated.
concept: When you apply carburettor heat, hot air from the exhaust manifold is drawn into the intake. This hot air is less dense than the cold ambient air you were using before. With lower air density comes fewer oxygen molecules per unit volume. Even though you've got the same volume of air flowing, there's less oxygen in it. The engine responds to this by producing less power—oxygen availability is a limiting factor in combustion. The magnitude of power loss depends on how hot the carb heat air is and how much of it you're using. At sea level on a cool day, the loss might be 5-8%. At altitude or on a hot day when the temperature difference is less pronounced, the loss might be minimal. This is normal and expected behaviour, not a malfunction. Pilots often apply carb heat on approach or in icing conditions and notice the RPM drop; that's exactly what should happen. You accept this power loss as the price of ice prevention. On a high-power flight, you'd avoid continuous carb heat to prevent unnecessarily sacrificing power.
exam_tip: Carb heat = hot air = less dense = fewer oxygen molecules = less power. Normal trade-off for ice prevention. Know it's expected and temporary.
key_facts: Hot air is less dense than cold air
Lower density = fewer oxygen molecules
Results in measurable power loss
5-8% loss typical at sea level
Acceptable trade for ice prevention
Not a malfunction or concern
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical source of heat for the carburettor hot air.
concept: Carburettor heat comes from the engine exhaust manifold, which is scorching hot after combustion—typically 400-600°C. The heat source is a shroud or ducting wrapped around the exhaust manifold (or sometimes around the engine cowling near the hot areas). A thermostatically controlled valve or simple lever-operated valve directs ram air (fresh cold air from the intake) through this hot shroud before sending it to the carburettor. The air passes through the hot metal, warming significantly, and enters the carb as hot air. Some systems use a thermostatic valve that automatically blends hot and cold air to maintain a target temperature; others are manual controls where you simply select 'heat on' or 'off.' The system is simple—no pumps, no electricity required (in most cases)—just physics: ram air passes near hot surfaces and picks up heat. This is why carb heat is most effective at speed (good ram air pressure) and why landing and low-speed approaches create risk (reduced ram air, less heating available if you need it).
exam_tip: Heat source: exhaust manifold shroud. Air passes through, picks up heat. System is mechanical/thermal, usually simple. That's why ram air helps carb heat effectiveness.
key_facts: Exhaust manifold is the heat source
Temperature 400-600°C
Air passes through shroud around manifold
Heats air before carb inlet
Thermostatic or manual control valve
No electricity or pumps typically
Effectiveness varies with ram air available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the principal differences between a fuel injection system and carburettor systems.
concept: Fuel injection sprays fuel directly into the cylinder (direct injection) or the intake port (indirect/continuous-flow injection), bypassing the venturi mixing that carbs do. Because fuel's injected under pressure rather than sucked by air flow, the system's less altitude-sensitive—fuel flow is pump-driven, not pressure-drop driven. There's no float chamber to slosh around in manoeuvres, so fuel delivery stays consistent through banking and turbulence. Carb ice is virtually eliminated because you're not sucking fuel through a cold venturi. Fuel distribution is more even across cylinders, so all cylinders run at the same mixture ratio. The system is more responsive to power changes because the injector reacts to electrical signals instantly, not waiting for mechanical venturi effects. The downside: complexity (pumps, computer controls, precision injectors) and cost. But the advantages are significant, which is why fuel injection is now standard on most modern aircraft. You'll see it on many training aircraft nowadays.
exam_tip: Injection vs carb: pressure-driven vs suction, even distribution, no carb ice, altitude-insensitive, faster response. Know the key advantages.
key_facts: Fuel injected under pressure, not sucked
No carburettor ice risk
Even fuel distribution to all cylinders
Altitude-insensitive fuel flow
Faster response to power changes
Complex, expensive, but superior performance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the following components in a basic fuel injection system: (a) delivery pump system
concept: The delivery pump in a fuel injection system receives fuel from the engine-driven fuel pump and meters it based on throttle position, mixture setting, and engine RPM. It creates the pressure needed to push fuel through the distribution system and ensures the correct total fuel flow for the current power setting.
exam_tip: The delivery pump meters total fuel flow based on throttle, mixture, and RPM inputs.
key_facts: Receives fuel from the engine-driven pump at regulated pressure
Meters fuel based on throttle position, mixture lever, and RPM
Creates the pressure to push fuel through the distribution system
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the following components in a basic fuel injection system: (b) distribution system
concept: The distribution system (flow divider) takes the metered fuel from the delivery pump and divides it equally among all cylinders. It ensures each cylinder receives the same amount of fuel regardless of its position on the engine. Even distribution is one of the key advantages of fuel injection over carburettors.
exam_tip: The flow divider ensures equal fuel distribution to all cylinders -- a key advantage over carburettors.
key_facts: Flow divider splits metered fuel equally to all cylinders
Ensures even fuel distribution regardless of cylinder position
More precise fuel metering than carburettor systems
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the following components in a basic fuel injection system: (c) injectors
concept: Injectors (or injector nozzles) are located at each cylinder's inlet port and spray a fine mist of fuel into the intake airstream just before it enters the cylinder. Each injector has a calibrated orifice to deliver a precise amount of fuel. The fuel is atomised as it passes through the nozzle, promoting thorough mixing with the incoming air.
exam_tip: Each cylinder has its own calibrated injector nozzle at the intake port -- one injector per cylinder.
key_facts: One calibrated injector per cylinder at the intake port
Atomises fuel into a fine spray for efficient mixing
Calibrated orifice ensures precise fuel delivery to each cylinder
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principal differences between continuous flow fuel injection, and direct fuel injection systems.
concept: Continuous-flow fuel injection (like the classic Lycoming TIO-series) sprays fuel continuously into the intake manifold, upstream of the intake valve. Fuel mixes with air in the manifold before entering the cylinder. Injectors run constantly at varying flow rates; throttle and power changes adjust flow pressure and amount. Direct fuel injection sprays fuel straight into the cylinder at high pressure, timed to fire just before or after the spark. Since injection happens in the combustion chamber rather than upstream, the fuel doesn't have time to vaporise before burning—you get very precise combustion control and slightly better efficiency. Direct injection also allows the injector timing to be coordinated with spark timing and piston position for optimum burn. Modern engines favour direct injection for this precision. Continuous-flow's simpler and was the first widely-adopted system, so you'll still see it on many aircraft. The key difference: timing and location—continuous-flow is untimed and upstream; direct is precisely timed and in-cylinder.
exam_tip: Continuous-flow: untimed, into intake manifold, simpler. Direct: precisely timed, into cylinder, efficient. Know where each injects.
key_facts: Continuous-flow: into intake manifold (upstream)
Untimed, fuel mixes in manifold
Simpler, still effective
Direct: into cylinder
Precisely timed to combustion event
Better efficiency, more control
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With regard to using fuel injection systems in aero-engines, explain the: (a) advantages
concept: Fuel injection advantages include: more even fuel distribution across all cylinders (no manifold bias), elimination of carburettor icing risk (no venturi to create cooling), better fuel economy (more precise metering), improved hot starting (no fuel pooling in a float chamber), and slightly more power from better mixture control.
exam_tip: Key fuel injection advantages: no carb ice risk, better fuel distribution, improved economy, and easier hot starts.
key_facts: No carburettor icing -- no venturi cooling effect
More even fuel distribution to all cylinders
Better fuel economy and slightly more power from precise metering
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With regard to using fuel injection systems in aero-engines, explain the: (b) disadvantages
concept: Fuel injection disadvantages include: difficulty starting when hot (vapour lock in fuel lines), more complex and expensive to maintain, harder to detect induction icing (no carb heat gauge), and potential for all cylinders to fail simultaneously if the fuel system fails (unlike a carburettor, which would fail more gradually).
exam_tip: Key disadvantage: vapour lock causing hard hot starts, and more complex/expensive maintenance.
key_facts: Vapour lock in fuel lines causes difficult hot starting
More complex and expensive to maintain than carburettors
Susceptible to complete fuel stoppage if the injection system fails
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the normal symptoms of intake ice formation, and the correct use of alternate air sources.
concept: Intake ice (different from carb ice) forms when supercooled water droplets in the air freeze directly onto the intake system components. Symptoms are similar to carb ice: rough running, power loss, and RPM fluctuation. With fuel injection, you don't have carb ice risk, but you can still get intake icing if moisture freezes on the intake filter or intake manifold. Some aircraft have alternate air sources—intakes that draw from inside the engine cowling (which is warmer from engine heat) instead of free air. Activating alternate air bypasses any ice on the external intake and draws from this protected source. On a carburettor aircraft, this is the carb heat control. On a fuel-injection aircraft, there might be a separate alternate air valve. The symptoms and fix are similar: if you notice rough running, power loss, or fluctuations in icing conditions, apply alternate air immediately. Modern aircraft with fuel injection sometimes don't need alternate air because they're less susceptible to icing, but many still have it as a backup.
exam_tip: Intake ice: symptoms like carb ice (rough, power loss). Fix: apply alternate air. Alternate air draws from cowling, bypasses external ice. On injection aircraft, might be separate valve or less critical.
key_facts: Intake ice: supercooled water freezes on intake components
Symptoms: rough, power loss, RPM fluctuation
Alternate air: draws from warm cowling area
Bypass external intake ice
Some injection aircraft have alternate air valve
Fix: activate alternate air in icing conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: With regard to exhaust driven turbo charging and mechanically driven supercharging, explain the: (a) advantages
concept: Supercharging and turbocharging both compress the intake air before it enters the cylinders, increasing air density and therefore power output. The main advantage is maintaining sea-level power at higher altitudes where air density would otherwise reduce performance. This increases the aircraft's critical altitude (full-throttle height) and improves climb and cruise performance.
exam_tip: Main advantage: maintains sea-level engine power at higher altitudes by compressing the intake air.
key_facts: Compresses intake air to increase air density and volumetric efficiency
Maintains sea-level power at higher altitudes
Turbochargers use exhaust gas energy; superchargers are mechanically driven from the engine
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With regard to exhaust driven turbo charging and mechanically driven supercharging, explain the: (b) disadvantages
concept: Disadvantages include increased engine complexity, weight, and maintenance costs. Compressing the intake air heats it, which increases detonation risk and may require an intercooler. Turbochargers add turbo lag and run at extreme temperatures requiring careful management. Over-boosting (exceeding maximum MAP) can damage the engine.
exam_tip: Key disadvantages: added complexity, detonation risk from hot compressed air, and over-boosting danger.
key_facts: Increased complexity, weight, and maintenance costs
Compressed air runs hotter -- increased detonation risk
Over-boosting (excessive MAP) can cause severe engine damage
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an exhaust driven turbocharger.
concept: Exhaust gases exiting the cylinders have significant thermal and kinetic energy. A turbocharger captures that energy. The hot exhaust passes through a turbine (similar to a small jet engine turbine) causing it to spin at very high RPM—up to 150,000+ RPM. This turbine is mechanically linked on the same shaft to a compressor that draws intake air and compresses it before sending it to the engine. The compressed air (boost) has higher density and pressure, so more oxygen's available per unit volume for combustion. Higher oxygen means the engine can burn more fuel and produce more power. Unlike a supercharger, the turbo doesn't draw power from the engine; it uses what would otherwise be wasted exhaust energy. The system's quite efficient. The downside: turbo lag from throttle application until the turbine spins up to speed, and high exhaust temperatures requiring cooling.
exam_tip: Exhaust spins turbine → turbine spins compressor → compressor pressurises intake air → more power. Simple concept, elegant system.
key_facts: Exhaust drives turbine at very high RPM
Turbine shaft drives compressor
Compressor pressurises intake air
Higher density air = more combustion power
Uses waste exhaust energy
Minimal parasitic power loss
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the differences between aviation gasoline (AVGAS) and motor gasoline (MOGAS).
concept: AVGAS (aviation gasoline, typically AVGAS 100LL for training and light aircraft) is specifically formulated for aircraft engines. It contains lead as an anti-knock additive (lead and bromine compounds (tetraethyl lead with ethylene dibromide as a scavenger)), which boosts octane rating. The 'LL' means 'low-lead'—newer formulation with reduced lead (about 0.56 grams per litre) compared to older formulations. AVGAS is manufactured to strict aviation standards (ASTM D910), with controlled volatility, purity, and octane. Motor gasoline (MOGAS) is fuel for cars—designed for automotive engines with different characteristics. MOGAS has much higher volatility (more prone to vapour lock in aircraft at altitude), typically lower octane (premium MOGAS might be 95 octane vs AVGAS 100), and contains ethanol (typically 5-10%) which can corrode fuel system components and separate from water in tanks. MOGAS's cheaper, but it's not approved for most aircraft engines unless specifically authorised by the manufacturer. Using MOGAS in an aircraft engine that's not approved for it can cause detonation, fouling, and fuel system damage. Always use the fuel grade specified in your aircraft's POH.
exam_tip: AVGAS: leads-containing (except unleaded variants), strict aviation spec, 100LL standard. MOGAS: automotive, cheaper, ethanol, not approved for most aircraft. Know the differences and why AVGAS is required.
key_facts: AVGAS 100LL: tetraethyl lead anti-knock, low-lead formula
ASTM D910 spec, controlled volatility
MOGAS: automotive fuel, higher volatility
MOGAS contains ethanol (5-10%)
MOGAS lower octane and cheaper
MOGAS not approved unless specified in POH
AVGAS 100/130 is dyed green; AVGAS 100LL is dyed blue
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain fuel octane ratings and performance numbers.
concept: Octane rating measures a fuel's resistance to detonation. The scale is based on two reference fuels: iso-octane (which resists detonation well, rated 100) and n-heptane (which detonates easily, rated 0). A fuel with a 100 octane rating behaves like iso-octane. In simple terms, higher octane = higher detonation resistance. AVGAS 100 means the fuel matches 100 octane iso-octane in anti-knock performance. Some high-octane fuels use a performance number system (PN), especially older military specifications, where the number can exceed 100 (like 115/145 PN). The first number is the lean mixture rating; the second is the rich mixture rating. Higher PN means better knock resistance, allowing higher power settings or compression ratios. Your aircraft's POH specifies the minimum octane required—use that grade or higher. Using lower octane risks detonation; using excessively higher octane (whilst not harmful) is wasteful. At places like Ardmore, you'll always use AVGAS 100LL unless your aircraft's approved for something different.
exam_tip: Octane rating = detonation resistance. 100 = iso-octane reference. Higher is better for high-compression engines. Know minimum spec for your aircraft.
key_facts: Octane scale based on iso-octane (100) and n-heptane (0)
Higher octane = more detonation resistance
Performance number: PN-115/145 format
Use minimum octane specified in POH
Higher octane than required is wasteful, not harmful
AVGAS 100LL standard for most training aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the likely result of using a higher grade, or a lower grade, of fuel than that recommended for a given aircraft.
concept: Using higher octane than recommended (e.g., 130/135 in an engine spec'd for 100/130): generally harmless. The engine will run slightly cooler because higher-octane fuel burns more slowly, which is fine. You might see marginally better performance at very high power or altitude, but it's minimal. The downside is cost—premium fuel's expensive, and you're wasting money for negligible benefit. Using lower octane than recommended (e.g., 80/87 in an engine spec'd for 100/130): dangerous. The fuel's more prone to detonation, and at the power settings your engine's designed for, it will knock. Sustained detonation damages pistons, valves, bearings, and rings. You lose power (knock forces you to reduce power), and the engine develops problems. Progressive damage from chronic low-octane usage eventually causes complete failure. If you're at Hamilton and only 80-octane's available, your only option is to operate at reduced power (climb more slowly, etc.) to stay below the detonation threshold. Better to not fly than to risk detonation damage.
exam_tip: Higher octane: safe, unnecessary cost. Lower octane: detonation risk, engine damage. Always use specified grade.
key_facts: Higher octane: safe, marginal benefit, unnecessary cost
Lower octane: detonation risk, progressive damage
Use minimum octane specified in POH
Reduced power operation if lower octane only available
Don't fly if proper fuel unavailable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the caution against using automobile fuel (MOGAS) in an aircraft engine, unless specifically authorised.
concept: MOGAS (automobile petrol) seems logical—it's cheaper and available everywhere. But there are critical issues. First, ethanol content (typically 5-10% in NZ pump fuel) causes corrosion in some aircraft fuel system components (rubber, seals, coatings) and can separate from water in tanks, creating a water layer that gums up fuel system. Second, volatility is too high—MOGAS boils at lower temperatures, creating vapour lock in aircraft fuel systems at altitude (fuel boils to vapour, blocks lines). Third, AVGAS is formulated for aviation use with strict quality controls; MOGAS is automotive and subject to different (less stringent) specs. Fourth, most aircraft engines are approved only for AVGAS; the fuel system and engine aren't validated for MOGAS. Insurance becomes invalid if you use unauthorised fuel and damage results. Some experimental or light-sport aircraft are specifically approved for MOGAS, and there's a whole STCed system for certain models, but that requires STC and POH approval. Unless your aircraft specifically says 'MOGAS approved' in writing, use AVGAS. The cost saving isn't worth the risk to the engine and systems.
exam_tip: MOGAS tempting but dangerous: ethanol corrosion, vapour lock, fuel system damage, engine damage. Caution: don't use unless explicitly approved in POH and STC.
key_facts: MOGAS contains ethanol (corrosion risk)
Higher volatility (vapour lock at altitude)
Not validated for aircraft engines
Insurance invalid if unauthorised fuel used
Some aircraft STC'd for MOGAS (rare)
Use AVGAS unless POH specifically allows MOGAS
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the distinguishing features of aviation turbine fuel (AVTUR/Jet A1).
concept: Aviation turbine fuel (Jet A1, also called AVTUR in some regions) is used in gas turbine engines (jets). It's a kerosene-like fuel, quite different from AVGAS. Jet A1 is less volatile than AVGAS—it boils at higher temperatures, so vapour lock isn't a concern. It's thicker, more like diesel, and flows more slowly. Colour is typically clear to pale straw (amber-ish), and it has a distinctive odour. Flash point is around 38-66°C depending on specification. It requires heating systems in the aircraft to prevent gelling at high altitude (very cold temperatures up there). Jet A1 contains no lead additives. You'll see it at major airports and training facilities with turbine aircraft. Never, ever use Jet fuel in a piston engine—it won't ignite properly in a piston engine's spark-plug ignition system, and it'll gum up everything. Piston engines absolutely require AVGAS.
exam_tip: Jet A1: turbine fuel, kerosene-like, no lead, less volatile, thicker. Different from AVGAS. Never mix them up.
key_facts: Jet A1: for gas turbine engines only
Kerosene-like consistency
Clear to pale straw colour
Flash point 38-66°C
No lead additives
Gels at very cold temps (requires heating)
Never use in piston engines
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: State the difference between the decals used on AVTUR and AVGAS fuelling equipment.
concept: Aviation facilities use colour-coded decals and labels on fuel equipment to prevent mixing fuels (which would be catastrophic). AVGAS fuelling equipment is marked with red decals/labels with white lettering—typically saying 'AVGAS' and listing the octane (100LL, 80/87, etc.). Jet fuel (AVTUR/Jet A1) equipment is marked with black decals/labels. This colour distinction makes it immediately obvious which fuel type you're accessing—red for petrol, black for jet. Some airports also use different hose connectors (AVGAS uses a certain size, Jet uses another) as an extra mechanical safety measure. Never ignore these labels. Using wrong fuel destroys engines and creates safety hazards. At any airport, the fuel colour and labels are your first check—always verify before opening the filler cap.
exam_tip: AVGAS equipment: red decals. Jet fuel equipment: black decals. Verify before fuelling. Never ignore colour coding.
key_facts: AVGAS: red decals/labels
Jet fuel: black decals/labels
Colour-coding prevents accidental mixing
Different hose connectors also prevent mix-up
Always verify colour before fuelling
Using wrong fuel = engine failure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions which can be taken to avoid fuel contamination with water and other impurities.
concept: Fuel contamination's a serious issue. Water can separate in fuel tanks (especially in humid climates like New Zealand's coasts), and if it reaches the engine, it stops combustion and causes corrosion. Dirt, dust, and microbial growth are other contaminants. Precautions: always use a fuel quality check before flight—sump the tanks (drain a small amount into a clear container) and visually inspect for water (appears as separate layer or cloudiness), dirt, or discolouration. Store aircraft with fuel caps tight and filler openings sealed to prevent rain ingress. Keep fuel supplier containers sealed. When fuelling, use filtered nozzles and never leave tanks open unattended. Regularly drain fuel sumps to remove accumulated water and sediment. At busy training bases like Ardmore, fuel trucks should be regularly tested for contamination. In humid conditions, use fuel additive (biocide) occasionally to prevent microbial growth. Never use fuel from unknown sources. And never trust 'it looks okay'—always sump and inspect.
exam_tip: Precautions: sump before flight, sealed storage, filtered nozzles, sealed caps, regular sump drains, avoid unknown fuel sources.
key_facts: Sump tanks before every flight
Check for water, dirt, discolouration
Sealed storage and caps prevent water ingress
Filtered nozzles during fuelling
Regular sump drains remove contamination
Biocide additive prevents microbial growth
Never use unknown fuel sources
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the special precautions which must be taken when fuelling from drum stock.
concept: Fuelling from drums (stored fuel containers) at remote strips or when main supply's unavailable requires extra care. Drums can have accumulated water and sediment; they're not quality-controlled like fuel trucks. Always inspect the drum exterior first—don't use rusty, damaged, or questionable drums. Before opening, ensure the drum's been stationary for several hours so sediment settles. Use a clean, sealed funnel with a fine mesh strainer when pouring fuel into the aircraft. Pour slowly and steadily to avoid splashing and contamination. Visually inspect the fuel as it flows—any cloudiness, water separation, or particles means stop immediately. Never fill aircraft to absolute maximum when using drums; leave some headspace to prevent overflow if temperature changes. Keep the area clean (no dust or dirt blowing around). After fuelling, immediately cap and seal the aircraft filler. And critically: sump the tanks before flight to catch any contamination that made it through. Drums are last resort; always prefer fuel trucks when available.
exam_tip: Drum fuelling: inspect drum, let sediment settle, use clean strainer, pour slowly, visually check fuel, sump before flight.
key_facts: Inspect drums for rust/damage
Let sediment settle before opening
Use fine-mesh strainer funnel
Pour slowly and watch for contamination
Visually inspect fuel colour/clarity
Never fill to absolute maximum
Sump tanks before flight
Prefer fuel trucks when available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for the avoidance of non-approved plastic containers.
concept: Non-approved plastic containers can leach chemicals into fuel (especially in warm conditions or with certain fuel types) and degrade, causing particles to contaminate the fuel. Some plastics absorb water through their walls, allowing moisture to enter the fuel. Approved fuel containers (aviation-specific metal or approved plastic) are designed to resist these issues. AVGAS can dissolve some plastics, causing failure; Jet fuel similarly attacks incompatible plastics. Unauthorised containers might not seal properly, allowing air exposure and oxidation of fuel. They can also generate static electricity (non-conductive plastics) during transfer, creating fire risk. And they're often not certified for aviation fuel handling, so if there's damage and fuel spill or contamination, liability's yours. Always use approved containers—either metal aviation fuel cans or containers specifically rated for AVGAS/Jet A1. They're not expensive and they're essential for fuel integrity and safety.
exam_tip: Non-approved plastic: leaches chemicals, absorbs water, degrades, generates static. Use approved containers only.
key_facts: Non-approved plastic can leach chemicals
Absorbs moisture through walls
AVGAS and Jet fuel dissolve incompatible plastics
Static electricity generation fire risk
Poor sealing allows oxidation
Always use approved aviation fuel containers
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the exhaust manifold.
concept: The exhaust manifold's a system of pipes that collects hot exhaust gases from individual cylinders and combines them into a single flow. Each cylinder has an exhaust port leading into the manifold. As each cylinder completes its power stroke, the exhaust valve opens and burnt gases are expelled into the manifold, which channels them toward the tail pipe (and eventually out the engine exhaust). The manifold's designed to accommodate the pressure pulses from each cylinder's exhaust stroke, minimizing back-pressure. Modern manifolds often have runners of different lengths to optimise scavenging (the removal of exhaust from one cylinder doesn't interfere with another). The manifold gets extremely hot—400-600°C—and in some aircraft, it's used as the heat source for carburettor heat. The manifold must be well-sealed; any leaks allow hot exhaust to escape into the engine compartment, creating fire risk and heat damage.
exam_tip: Manifold collects exhaust from cylinders, combines into single flow, very hot. Seal integrity critical for safety.
key_facts: Collects exhaust from all cylinders
Channels to tail pipe/engine exhaust
Extremely hot (400-600°C)
Used for carb heat source
Must be properly sealed
Back-pressure affects scavenging
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of proper sealing of the exhaust manifold.
concept: The exhaust manifold operates at very high temperatures and pressure. If it leaks, hot gases escape into the engine compartment, creating several hazards. First, fire risk: 400-600°C exhaust in contact with fuel lines, hydraulic fluid, or electrical insulation causes fires. Second, asphyxiation risk: carbon monoxide (CO) can leak into the cabin if the manifold-to-cabin seal fails; CO's odourless and deadly. Third, corrosion: hot exhaust exposure degrades nearby components. Fourth, performance loss: any leak reduces back-pressure, which affects cylinder scavenging and power output. A cracked or loose manifold is a serious maintenance issue. You'd check for discolouration (heat damage) around manifold joints, listen for leaks during run-up, and look for any visible cracks during inspection. Poor seal integrity isn't something you patch; it requires repair or replacement. At pre-flight, if you notice discolouration or smell anything weird (burnt metal, unidentified odours), investigate the manifold.
exam_tip: Proper sealing prevents: fire, CO ingestion, corrosion, performance loss. Cracked manifold is maintenance item, not something to ignore.
key_facts: Prevents hot exhaust escape and fire
Prevents carbon monoxide ingress
Preserves system back-pressure
Protects nearby components from heat
Cracks require repair/replacement
Not a field-fix item
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the possible sources, indications and associated danger of carbon monoxide gas.
concept: Carbon monoxide (CO) is a colourless, odourless poisonous gas produced by incomplete combustion in the engine. Sources: exhaust manifold cracks or loose joints allowing CO into the cabin, defective cabin heater systems that route manifold heat into the cabin but allow gas mixing, or propane heaters in closed cabins. Indications of CO poisoning in the cockpit: headache (often the first sign), dizziness, nausea, flu-like symptoms, and in severe cases, loss of consciousness. The insidious part: CO symptoms resemble hypoxia, so pilots sometimes respond incorrectly (thinking it's altitude when it's poisoning). CO at high altitude is particularly dangerous because hypoxia masks it. Dangers: complete unconsciousness and death if exposure continues. Prevention: ensure manifold's properly sealed (no leaks), cabin heaters properly maintained, and any propane heater properly vented outside. Some aircraft have CO detectors (colour-changing patches or electronic) which change colour if CO's present—these are helpful safety items. If you suspect CO poisoning in flight: open all windows, close cabin heat (shut off the source), descend, and land immediately. Never ignore symptoms that might be CO.
exam_tip: CO: colourless, odourless, from exhaust leaks. Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea. Response: open windows, close heat, land. Prevent with manifold sealing.
key_facts: CO from incomplete combustion
Leaks from manifold cracks/joints
Colourless and odourless
Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea
Resembles hypoxia symptoms
Detection: colour-changing detectors
Prevention: manifold sealing, heater maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for fitting independent dual ignition systems to engines.
concept: Aircraft engines use two independent magnetos (not one big one) for several critical reasons. First, redundancy: if one magneto fails, the other continues firing the plugs, so you've still got partial power and can keep flying (though degraded). Second, safety: losing ignition means losing the engine; dual ignition significantly reduces that risk. Third, reliability: magnetos are mechanical and wear; two independent systems spread the risk. Fourth, better engine scavenging and power: each magneto fires its own set of spark plugs, so you get two sparks per power stroke (one from each magneto), creating a broader burn and more complete combustion. This improves power output and efficiency. Fifth, improved starting: with two magnetos firing, starting's easier. The trade-off: complexity and cost, but the reliability and safety benefits are worth it. Every piston trainer you fly has dual mags. The ignition switch typically has positions: off, left mag only, right mag only, and both. During magneto checks before flight, you test each mag independently to ensure both are functioning.
exam_tip: Dual mags: redundancy if one fails, better combustion (two sparks), improved starting. Why aircraft don't use single large magneto.
key_facts: Redundancy: one fails, one continues
Better combustion from dual sparks
Improved starting capability
Increases safety and reliability
Each mag fires its own plugs
Tested independently during run-up
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: List the essential components of an ignition system.
concept: An ignition system starts with the magneto (generates electrical power and timing). The magneto produces high-tension (HT) output that goes to a distributor (routes the spark to the right cylinder at the right time). From there, ignition harness (high-tension leads, aka spark plug wires) carries the spark to each spark plug. The spark plugs (in each cylinder) create the actual spark gap where ignition occurs. An impulse coupling (mechanical device) on the magneto ensures a hot spark during starting by retarding spark timing momentarily. Some systems also include a starting vibrator that helps establish initial oscillation. Capacitors in the magneto and wiring help sharpen the spark. The magneto itself contains a rotating magnet, coils, and electrical components that generate and time the spark. All these components work together; failure of any single component means loss of ignition and loss of that magneto (hence the need for dual systems).
exam_tip: Magneto, distributor, ignition harness, spark plugs, impulse coupling. Know each component's role.
key_facts: Magneto: power generation and timing
Distributor: routes spark to cylinders
Ignition harness: spark plug wires
Spark plugs: create ignition spark
Impulse coupling: aids starting
Capacitors: sharpen spark
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following: (a) an aircraft magneto
concept: An aircraft magneto generates its own high-voltage spark independently of the aircraft's electrical system. A permanent magnet rotates past a coil (primary winding), inducing a current. When this current is interrupted by breaker points, the collapsing magnetic field induces a very high voltage in the secondary winding (thousands of volts), which fires the spark plug. Two independent magnetos per engine provide redundancy.
exam_tip: Magnetos are self-contained and independent of the aircraft battery -- the engine keeps running even with total electrical failure.
key_facts: Self-powered: permanent magnet + coils generate high voltage independently
Breaker points interrupt primary current to induce high voltage in secondary winding
Two independent magnetos per engine for redundancy
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following: (b) the distributor
concept: The distributor directs the high-voltage spark from the magneto to each spark plug in the correct firing order. It's a rotating contact that connects the magneto's high-tension output to the correct cylinder's spark plug lead at precisely the right moment. The distributor rotates at camshaft speed (half crankshaft speed).
exam_tip: The distributor routes the magneto's high-voltage output to the correct spark plug in firing order sequence.
key_facts: Routes high-voltage spark to the correct cylinder in sequence
Rotates at camshaft speed (half crankshaft RPM)
One distributor per magneto
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following: (c) ignition harness (high tension leads)
concept: The ignition harness (high-tension leads) carries the high-voltage current from the distributor to each spark plug. These leads must be heavily insulated and shielded to prevent electrical interference with avionics and radios. They must withstand voltages of 15,000-25,000 volts without arcing or leaking.
exam_tip: Ignition leads must be heavily shielded to prevent radio interference from the high-voltage spark.
key_facts: Carry 15,000-25,000 volts from distributor to spark plugs
Heavily insulated and shielded to prevent radio interference
Must be kept in good condition -- cracked insulation causes misfiring and radio noise
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following: (d) spark plugs
concept: Spark plugs provide two electrodes separated by a small gap inside the combustion chamber. When the magneto's high voltage reaches the plug, a spark jumps the gap and ignites the compressed fuel-air mixture. Aircraft use two plugs per cylinder for redundancy and more complete combustion. Plugs can foul with carbon (too rich) or lead deposits.
exam_tip: Spark plug fouling: carbon deposits from rich mixture, lead deposits from leaded fuel -- both cause misfiring.
key_facts: Two per cylinder, fired by separate magnetos
Spark jumps the electrode gap to ignite the mixture
Can foul with carbon (rich running) or lead (from leaded AVGAS)
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following: (e) impulse couplings
concept: An impulse coupling is a spring-loaded device on one magneto that retards the spark timing during engine cranking and then snaps the magneto rotor forward rapidly to produce a strong spark. At low cranking speeds, a normal magneto produces a weak spark with too-advanced timing. The impulse coupling solves both problems -- it delays the spark and makes it much hotter.
exam_tip: Impulse coupling retards timing and boosts spark strength during cranking -- only one magneto has it.
key_facts: Spring mechanism retards spark timing during engine start
Snaps the rotor forward for a strong, hot spark at low cranking speed
Disengages automatically at normal operating RPM
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of fitting an impulse coupling to a magneto.
concept: During engine start, the starter motor cranks the engine very slowly (perhaps 50-100 RPM). At that speed, the magneto's rotating magnet spins slowly, generating very weak voltage—barely enough to fire the spark plugs reliably. You'd get a weak, late spark, making starting difficult or impossible. An impulse coupling solves this by mechanically retarding spark timing during start. As the engine cranks slowly, the impulse coupling holds back the magneto's rotor (delays its rotation slightly), allowing magnetic field to build up more before the spark fires. When the spark finally does occur, it's much hotter and more reliable. Once the engine fires and speed increases above normal idle RPM, centrifugal weights inside the impulse coupling overcome the retard mechanism, and the coupling disengages. Normal spark advance then applies automatically. So the impulse coupling's purpose: ensure hot, reliable spark during the slow cranking of engine start, then get out of the way once the engine's running. Without it, cold starting would be much harder.
exam_tip: Impulse coupling retards timing during slow crank, ensuring hot spark for starting. Disengages once engine speeds up. Why starting's reliable.
key_facts: Retards spark timing during start
Allows field buildup for hotter spark
Engages at slow crank speed
Disengages around 1000 RPM
Ensures reliable cold start
Automatically managed, pilot doesn't control it
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason ignition is timed to occur before top dead centre (TDC).
concept: Combustion doesn't happen instantaneously. When the spark plug fires, it ignites the fuel-air mixture, but the flame front takes several milliseconds to propagate through the combustion chamber. If you waited until the piston reached top dead centre (TDC) to fire the spark, by the time combustion developed, the piston would already be starting downward, and you'd waste the combustion energy. Instead, spark fires before TDC (typically 20-30 degrees before), so by the time the piston reaches TDC and starts its power stroke downward, combustion is fully developed and creates maximum pressure right when you need it—pushing the piston hard from the very start of the downstroke. This is called 'spark advance.' The amount of advance varies with engine speed and operating conditions; faster engines need more advance (longer lead time for combustion), slower engines need less. Modern high-altitude engines might have 35+ degrees of advance at high RPM. Getting spark timing right is critical for efficiency and power.
exam_tip: Spark before TDC because combustion takes time. Peak pressure needed right at start of power stroke = maximum force = most power.
key_facts: Spark before TDC = spark advance
Allows time for combustion development
Peak pressure at right moment = max power
Advance increases with engine speed
Typical 20-35 degrees before TDC
Critical for efficiency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the ignition switch(es) in the cockpit.
concept: The ignition switch is typically a 4-position rotary switch (sometimes 5 on complex aircraft): OFF, LEFT MAG, RIGHT MAG, and BOTH. OFF: both magnetos are grounded and disabled; no ignition. LEFT MAG: left magneto only is active; right magneto is grounded. RIGHT MAG: right magneto only is active; left magneto is grounded. BOTH: both magnetos active—normal flight operation. (Some aircraft have a START position where the magnetos and starter motor are energised together.) During start, you'd typically place the switch in BOTH, and the starter motor cranks the engine while both magnetos provide spark. Once running, you stay in BOTH. During magneto checks, you momentarily switch to LEFT or RIGHT individually to test each one independently. The spark plug wires and distributor are wired so that each magneto's spark always goes to the correct plugs; the switch just controls power to each magneto. A failed magneto or switch would leave one magneto active (the other grounded), giving you asymmetrical ignition—rough running but still flight-capable.
exam_tip: OFF, LEFT, RIGHT, BOTH positions. BOTH for normal flight. LEFT/RIGHT for testing. OFF for security. Know the switch function.
key_facts: OFF: both grounded, no ignition
LEFT: left mag active, right grounded
RIGHT: right mag active, left grounded
BOTH: both active (normal flight)
START: adds starter motor (some aircraft)
Used for mag checking and starting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Outline the correct procedures to be followed during magneto checks.
concept: Before every flight, you perform magneto checks during the run-up to ensure both magnetos are functioning correctly. Procedure: first, run the engine at a safe RPM (typically 1000-1500 RPM, as specified in the POH). Ensure the aircraft is in a stable configuration (trim set, flight surfaces free). With the engine running smoothly at BOTH, note the RPM. Slowly move the ignition switch to LEFT MAG and note the RPM drop. The drop should be modest—typically 50-150 RPM depending on the aircraft. Check that the drop is smooth and the engine doesn't surge or miss badly. Return to BOTH and wait for the engine to stabilise. Repeat with RIGHT MAG. The right-side RPM drop might be slightly different from the left (that's normal, as magnetos aren't perfectly matched). Critical checks: the RPM drop on each side shouldn't exceed the aircraft's limits (typically 150 RPM, but check the POH). The difference between left and right drops shouldn't exceed about 50 RPM. If one mag is significantly worse than the other, or if there's rough running, surging, or backfiring, there's a problem—don't fly. Also check that returning to BOTH smoothly increases RPM back to the original reading. Never test magnetos with a passenger unfamiliar with the procedure; they might get alarmed at the RPM drop.
exam_tip: Mag checks: smooth RPM drop on each side (50-150 RPM typical). Right-left difference max ~50 RPM. No surging. Back to BOTH should restore RPM smoothly.
key_facts: Perform at 1000-1500 RPM stable setting
Test LEFT, then RIGHT individually
Note RPM drop on each side
Drop should be smooth and modest
Right-left difference max ~50 RPM
Return to BOTH should restore RPM
Rough or excessive drop = maintenance issue
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications of various problems manifested during a magneto check.
concept: During mag checks, you observe several potential problems. Excessive RPM drop (more than 150 RPM on either side, or asymmetric—right much worse than left): indicates magneto degradation, worn brushes, or carbon buildup. Rough running or missing when on one mag: spark plugs might be fouled, or the magneto's misfiring. Surging (RPM rises and falls irregularly): might indicate a bad capacitor or intermittent contact in the magneto. Backfiring (popping sound from the exhaust): timing issue or magneto delivering spark at wrong time. Inability to return to smooth operation after mag check: again suggests magneto or plug issues. A dead mag (no RPM drop on one side, or massive drop close to shut-down): magneto's completely failed. Any of these warrant maintenance investigation. Some issues (fouled spark plugs from over-rich running) can be cleared by leaning and running at higher power for a bit; others (internal magneto failure) require replacement. Never ignore mag check problems—they indicate a safety issue that needs addressing before flight.
exam_tip: Excessive drop, asymmetry, rough, surge, backfire, dead mag = problems. Know which are critical (mag failure) vs potentially clearable (fouled plugs).
key_facts: Excessive RPM drop: magneto wear
Asymmetry: one mag worse than other
Rough/missing: fouled plugs or misfire
Surging: capacitor or contact issues
Backfiring: timing problem
Dead mag: complete failure
Some clearable, some need maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of spark plug fouling and the methods of clearing such fouling.
concept: Spark plug fouling happens when the spark gap becomes blocked by deposits, preventing the spark from jumping. Causes: running too rich (excess fuel creates carbon deposits and wets the plug), operating at low power for extended periods (plug doesn't get hot enough to self-clean), cold-starting (rich mixture to aid starting leaves deposits), or aged spark plugs (gap widens, deposits accumulate). Fouled plugs create a no-spark condition and misfire, rough running, and power loss. Clearing methods: lean the mixture (reduce fuel flow to burn off deposits and let plugs heat up), increase power (climb to cruise power where plugs self-clean—the hotter the operating temperature, the better plugs clean), or rough-running procedures (some POHs specify running at higher RPM to blow out fouling). If fouling persists, the plugs need actual cleaning or replacement by maintenance. At Hamilton or Christchurch, if you start cold and run too rich too long, you might foul plugs before even taking off; the fix is to lean and run at higher power to self-clean. Regular proper mixture management prevents fouling.
exam_tip: Over-rich = fouling. Fix: lean mixture, climb to cruise power for self-cleaning. Know why proper mixture management's important.
key_facts: Over-rich running = primary cause
Low-power operation = deposits accumulate
Fouling blocks spark gap
Symptoms: misfire, rough, power loss
Fix: lean mixture, increase power
Plugs self-clean at higher operating temp
Persistent fouling needs maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the components of a typical solid state ignition system.
concept: Solid state ignition replaces magnetos and mechanical contact points with electronic components. Key parts: electronic control unit (ECU, a small computer), power supply (battery or alternator), sensor(s) to detect engine speed and piston position (usually a magnetic pickup near the crankshaft), capacitor discharge module (stores and releases high voltage), ignition coil(s), and spark plugs. Some systems have dual ECUs and dual coils for redundancy (like dual mags). The ECU calculates the ideal spark timing based on engine speed, manifold pressure, and other parameters, then signals the coil to fire at exactly the right moment. No mechanical timing, no moving parts in the ignition circuit—just electrons. Because of this, reliability's very high (no brushes to wear, no contacts to pit). The system's dependent on electrical power; if battery or alternator fails, ignition fails (unlike magnetos, which generate their own power). Some aircraft have backup power systems to protect against total electrical failure.
exam_tip: Solid state: ECU, sensors, coil, no mechanical parts. Reliable, dependent on electrics. Know components and why it's different from magnetos.
key_facts: Electronic control unit (ECU)
Engine speed/position sensors
Power supply (battery/alternator)
Capacitor discharge module
Ignition coil(s)
Spark plugs
No mechanical timing components
Highly reliable if power available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical solid state ignition system.
concept: Operation is elegant in its simplicity from the pilot's perspective. The sensor (magnetic pickup) detects crankshaft rotation and sends signals to the ECU. The ECU processes these signals and any other inputs (manifold pressure, engine temperature, etc.) to calculate ideal spark timing. It then sends an electrical command to the coil(s), which builds up voltage and releases it (capacitor discharge) to the spark plugs at precisely the right moment. The timing's automatically optimised for current conditions—high power gets more advance, low power less advance, etc. From the flight deck, you don't think about spark timing; you just fly. The system handles it. Diagnostics: some systems have built-in self-tests that run when you turn on the master switch; annunciators or LEDs indicate system health. Failure modes are typically binary: system works or it doesn't (unlike magnetos, where degradation is gradual). If the ECU senses a sensor failure or other problem, it might go into 'limp home' mode where it defaults to a safe basic timing for limited operation.
exam_tip: Sensor → ECU → calculates timing → signals coil → spark. Automatic, optimised. Pilot doesn't manually adjust spark timing.
key_facts: Sensor signals ECU of crankshaft position
ECU calculates ideal timing
Commands coil to fire at right moment
Timing automatically optimised for conditions
No pilot adjustment of spark timing
Self-test capability
Usually binary: works or doesn't
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose for conducting typical solid state ignition integrity checks.
concept: Solid state systems include built-in self-diagnostics. Integrity checks verify that the system's functioning: all components are communicating, the sensors are detecting properly, the coil's charging, and the spark plugs are firing. These checks catch failures before flight. Purpose: ensure the ignition system's operational and safe. Unlike magneto checks (where you're listening to the engine's response), solid state checks often involve a test switch or automatic self-test that runs when you activate master switch. The system's either green-light good or flashing a fault code. This reduces ambiguity (no interpretation needed like with mag checks) and catches problems earlier. If a check fails, you don't fly until maintenance resolves it.
exam_tip: Purpose: verify system's operational before flight. Catches failures early. Automatic or test-switch based.
key_facts: Built-in self-test capability
Verifies all components communication
Detects sensor, coil, plug issues
Usually automatic on master activation
Clear pass/fail indication
Prevents flying with degraded system
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedure for conducting typical solid state ignition integrity checks.
concept: Exact procedures vary by system, so always consult the POH. General procedure: activate master switch; the system self-test runs automatically (usually a few seconds). LED indicators or annunciators show result—green/OK light means system's healthy; red/fault light means problem. Some systems allow manual test by pressing a test button; you'd hold it briefly and watch for the correct response (spark at plugs, coil responding, etc.). On more basic systems, you'd simply observe that the system powers up correctly when master's on and instruments show normal readings. Some aircraft also have a ground-check procedure: with master on and engine off, you can verify certain system functions without running. Once you start the engine, the system's continuously self-monitoring during flight. If a problem develops, an annunciator or warning light alerts you. Never skip these checks; solid state systems depend on power and electronics, so verifying they're healthy before flight is essential.
exam_tip: Check POH for exact procedure. Usually: master on, observe self-test, verify pass. No major interpretation like mag checks.
key_facts: Master on activates self-test
Observe LED/annunciator indication
Manual test button on some systems
Green = OK, red/fault = problem
System continuously monitors in flight
Follow POH exactly
Don't fly if test fails
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With reference to a solid-state ignition system: (a) explain the significance of maintaining the ignition power supply
concept: Solid-state (electronic) ignition systems depend on an external power supply (usually the aircraft's electrical bus) rather than magneto self-generation. If the power supply is lost, the ignition system fails completely. Maintaining a reliable power supply is therefore critical -- unlike magnetos, which are self-powered and keep running even in a total electrical failure.
exam_tip: Solid-state ignition depends on external power -- unlike magnetos, it fails if the electrical supply is lost.
key_facts: Requires external electrical power to operate
Loss of power supply = loss of ignition (unlike self-powered magnetos)
Power supply integrity is critical for flight safety
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With reference to a solid-state ignition system: (b) describe a typical power supply backup system
concept: A typical backup for solid-state ignition uses a dedicated battery or capacitor bank that can power the ignition system independently if the main bus fails. Some systems use a dual power supply arrangement where the ignition can draw from either the main bus or an emergency bus. This provides redundancy similar to the independence of traditional magnetos.
exam_tip: Backup systems include dedicated batteries or emergency bus connections to maintain ignition if the main bus fails.
key_facts: Dedicated backup battery or capacitor provides independent ignition power
Some systems use dual bus feeds for redundancy
Backup must be tested during pre-flight checks
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the functioning of the starter motor.
concept: A starter motor's an electric motor powered by the aircraft battery. When you engage the starter (via a cockpit switch), battery current flows through a solenoid (electromagnet relay). The solenoid pulls a mechanical linkage that engages a gear on the starter shaft with a larger gear on the engine's flywheel. Simultaneously, the solenoid completes a heavy-current circuit that powers the motor itself. The motor's armature (rotating coil) spins in a magnetic field, creating torque. This torque, multiplied by the gear ratio (small gear on starter, large gear on flywheel), provides enough force to crank the engine over against compression resistance. Once the engine fires and speed exceeds starter speed, a one-way clutch (sprag clutch) in the starter gear disengages—the engine stops driving the starter back. Current ceases (you've released the starter switch), and the solenoid releases, disengaging the gear. Simple but effective.
exam_tip: Solenoid engages gear and powers motor. Motor drives flywheel gear. One-way clutch releases after engine start.
key_facts: Battery powers starter motor via solenoid
Solenoid engages starter gear to flywheel
Electric motor creates cranking torque
Gear reduction multiplies torque
One-way clutch disengages after start
Solenoid releases when starter switch released
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operational limitations of the starter motor.
concept: Starter motors draw enormous current—several hundred amps—from the battery. This is only sustainable briefly; prolonged cranking drains the battery quickly. Typical cranking duration: 5-10 seconds maximum. If the engine doesn't catch in that time, you release the starter, wait 30 seconds to let the battery recover, then try again. Multiple rapid starts in succession can completely drain the battery. Starter motors also generate intense heat during operation. Continuous operation (beyond 10 seconds) can overheat the armature and damage windings. POH specifies maximum continuous crank time (often 10 seconds) and mandatory cool-down periods between attempts. Starter motors are designed for intermittent duty, not continuous running.
