exam_tip: Below the CS range, blades are on the fine pitch stops -- the throttle controls both RPM and MAP like a fixed-pitch prop.
key_facts: Blades are against the fine pitch stops
Governor cannot reduce pitch further -- propeller acts like fixed pitch
Throttle controls both RPM and MAP directly
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain how the constant-speed unit (CSU) acts to change the pitch of the blades and maintain rpm with changes: (a) to power
concept: When power (throttle) is increased, more torque is applied to the propeller, causing RPM to try to increase. The governor senses the RPM increase and directs oil to coarsen the propeller blades, increasing their load (propeller torque) to match the new engine torque. RPM returns to the selected value but at a coarser blade angle.
exam_tip: Increase power: RPM tries to rise, governor coarsens blades to maintain RPM. RPM stays the same, MAP increases.
key_facts: Increased throttle = more engine torque = RPM tries to rise
Governor senses overspeed and coarsens blades
RPM returns to set value; MAP increases, blade angle increases
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain how the constant-speed unit (CSU) acts to change the pitch of the blades and maintain rpm with changes: (b) in airspeed
concept: When airspeed increases (such as in a descent), the propeller's angle of attack decreases, reducing propeller torque and allowing RPM to rise. The governor senses this and coarsens the blades to restore the original RPM. Conversely, if airspeed decreases (in a climb), the governor fines the blades to prevent RPM from dropping. The CSU constantly adjusts to maintain the selected RPM.
exam_tip: Speed increase: governor coarsens blades to prevent overspeed. Speed decrease: governor fines blades to prevent underspeed.
key_facts: Airspeed increase reduces blade angle of attack, RPM tries to rise
Governor coarsens blades to maintain RPM
Airspeed decrease: governor fines blades to maintain RPM
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the method of operation of typical pitch changing mechanisms.
concept: Pitch-changing mechanisms are all hydraulically actuated. You've got a piston or diaphragm inside the hub that moves along the blade axis. When hydraulic oil enters on one side (from the CSU governor or pilot control), the piston pushes the blades fine. Springs or mechanical stops push back. The oil flow's metered through orifices so the blades change pitch smoothly over a few seconds rather than snapping instantly. Some designs use a rotating swashplate that converts circular motion into linear motion—the plate angle controls how much the piston moves per blade rotation. Most light-twin props use a simpler spool-valve design where oil routing directly controls piston position. The whole mechanism lives inside the prop hub, sealed and out of sight. From the cockpit, you just feel the prop lever; the hydraulics do the work. If the hydraulic system fails, springs usually spring the blades to a safe default position (usually coarse pitch, which is safest).
exam_tip: Hydraulic piston or diaphragm changes pitch. Governor or pilot controls oil routing. Springs provide safety backup.
key_facts: Hydraulic piston or diaphragm moves inside propeller hub
Governor-controlled valve meters oil to change pitch
Springs provide restoring force and safety backup
Metering orifices control rate of pitch change
Fine pitch = hydraulic pressure pushes piston inward
Coarse pitch = springs push piston outward (or governor vents pressure)
Rotating swashplate or spool valve routes hydraulic oil
System is sealed within propeller hub
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Hartzell/McCauley technical manuals

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical CSU governor.
concept: The CSU governor is a mechanical valve controlled by centrifugal weights. Inside the governor housing you've got two or more heavy flyweights on pivot arms. As the propeller spins, these weights want to fly outward due to centrifugal force. The faster the spin, the more outward force. As the weights move, they control a spool valve. When RPM's low, weights hang inward; the valve allows hydraulic oil (pressurised by the engine-driven pump) to flow into the prop hub, pitching the blades finer. When RPM climbs to your set value, the outward centrifugal force balances a spring tension, and the valve reaches a neutral position—no oil flows in or out of the hub, pitch holds steady. If RPM climbs higher, weights push the valve to allow oil to drain from the hub (or block supply), pitching blades coarser. The setting spring tension defines your desired RPM. You adjust it by turning the propeller control in the cockpit. All this happens at propeller speed (hundreds of times per second for multi-blade props), so the governor constantly 'hunts' for equilibrium—it's incredibly responsive.
exam_tip: Centrifugal weights vs. spring tension balance to control oil flow. Fast adjustment = quick RPM regulation. Loss of oil pressure = loss of governor function.
key_facts: Centrifugal weights pivot outward with increasing RPM
Spring tension opposes centrifugal force
Spool valve routes hydraulic oil based on weight position
Low RPM: oil flows into hub (pitch finer)
High RPM: oil drains or supply blocked (pitch coarser)
Set RPM determined by governor spring tension
Propeller lever in cockpit adjusts spring tension
Governor responds continuously at engine speed frequency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Hartzell/McCauley governor design

syllabus_desc: Explain the power management requirements as the propeller blades are travelling from the normal thrust to the reverse thrust angle.
concept: Reverse pitch on a twin-engine aircraft is powerful braking—you land, reverse one or both props, and you're essentially using thrust to slow down. But you've got to manage power carefully. First, only reverse the prop when the airframe's firmly on the ground and weight's on the wheels (not in the air, or you'll have a stall). Second, reduce throttle to idle before you start the reversal—you don't want to push power into the reverse-pitch prop or you'll shock-load the engine and get a backfire. The prop will be pulling/pushing backward if you've got power in it. You move the prop lever fully aft (past the coarse-pitch stop into the reverse-pitch range), and the governor takes the prop into reverse pitch. As reverse pitch increases, the prop produces backward thrust. You modulate the throttle to control reversing power—same as forward flight, throttle controls manifold pressure. On some aircraft, reaching reverse pitch locks the throttle at a safe advance (you can't overspeed). Once you've slowed on the runway, you return the prop lever to forward, which brings the blades back to fine pitch. Then reduce throttle. Never try to taxi or take off with props in reverse pitch.
exam_tip: Reduce power to idle before reversing. Use throttle to modulate reverse power. Return to forward pitch before attempting taxi or takeoff.
key_facts: Only reverse pitch with weight on wheels (not in air)
Reduce throttle to idle before moving prop lever to reverse
Moving prop lever aft past coarse-pitch stop engages reverse-pitch mode
Throttle controls manifold pressure in reverse pitch (as in forward flight)
Governor controls pitch angle within reverse-pitch range
Reverse thrust used for emergency braking on landing
Return prop lever to forward pitch before attempting to taxi
Never use reverse pitch for taxi or normal ground movement
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Piper Aztec POH reverse-thrust procedures

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal handling of the propeller pitch and power controls for a two lever control system: (a) when the aircraft is on the ground
concept: On the ground with a two-lever system, the propeller should be at full fine pitch (prop lever fully forward). This gives the lightest propeller load for engine starting and ground operations. For engine run-up, set the required RPM with the throttle and check the prop by briefly pulling the pitch lever back to confirm the governor is working (RPM should drop).
exam_tip: On the ground: prop fully fine (lever forward). During run-up, cycle the prop to check governor operation.
key_facts: Prop lever fully forward (fine pitch) for all ground operations
Fine pitch gives lightest load for starting and ground idle
Cycle prop lever during run-up to check governor function
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal handling of the propeller pitch and power controls for a two lever control system: (b) for 'exercising' the CSU
concept: Exercising the CSU involves cycling the prop lever from fine to coarse and back a few times during the run-up. This circulates warm oil through the governor and propeller hub, ensuring the system responds properly. Cold, thick oil can make the governor sluggish. You should see RPM drop when you move toward coarse and recover when you return to fine.
exam_tip: Exercise the CSU during run-up: cycle fine to coarse a few times. Watch for RPM drop and recovery.
key_facts: Circulates warm oil through the governor and propeller hub
Cycle fine to coarse 2-3 times during run-up
RPM should drop when coarsening and recover when returning to fine
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal handling of the propeller pitch and power controls for a two lever control system: (c) prior to take-off and landing
concept: Prior to take-off, set prop lever fully fine (forward) for maximum RPM and take-off power. For landing, set prop fully fine during the approach so that if you need to go around, the propeller is already configured for maximum power and climb. Full fine also gives maximum propeller braking (drag) on the landing roll.
exam_tip: Full fine (lever forward) for take-off AND approach/landing -- ready for go-around and provides prop braking on rollout.
key_facts: Full fine for take-off: maximum RPM and power available
Full fine for approach/landing: ready for go-around
Fine pitch provides maximum propeller drag on the landing roll
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal handling of the propeller pitch and power controls for a two lever control system: (d) when increasing or decreasing power in the air
concept: When increasing power in flight, always increase RPM (prop lever) first, then increase MAP (throttle). This prevents overboosting the engine with a coarse (low RPM) prop setting. When reducing power, reduce MAP (throttle) first, then reduce RPM (prop lever). The rule: RPM before MAP up, MAP before RPM down -- or simply, 'prop before power up, power before prop down'.
exam_tip: Power increase: RPM first, then throttle. Power decrease: throttle first, then RPM. Prevents overboosting.
key_facts: Increasing: prop (RPM) forward first, then throttle (MAP) forward
Decreasing: throttle back first, then prop back
Prevents high MAP with low RPM (overboosting risk)
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal handling of the power control for a single lever control system: (a) when the aircraft is on the ground
concept: A single lever control system combines the throttle and prop control into one power lever. On the ground, advancing the lever increases both power and RPM automatically. The pilot doesn't need to manage the prop independently -- the system is designed to optimise the RPM/MAP combination for any lever position.
exam_tip: Single lever: one control manages both power and RPM. Simpler cockpit management than the two-lever system.
key_facts: One power lever controls both throttle and prop RPM
System automatically optimises RPM/MAP combination
Simpler pilot workload than managing separate levers
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal handling of the power control for a single lever control system: (b) for 'exercising' the CSU
concept: Exercising the CSU with a single lever system is done by cycling the power lever through a range and observing that RPM responds correctly. Some single lever systems have a separate 'exercise' procedure outlined in the POH. The purpose is the same -- circulate warm oil through the governor and hub.
exam_tip: Single lever CSU exercise: follow the specific POH procedure -- the principle is the same as two-lever (warm the oil).
key_facts: Follow the specific POH procedure for your aircraft type
Purpose: warm oil through governor and propeller hub
Observe that RPM responds correctly through the power range
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal handling of the power control for a single lever control system: (c) prior to take-off and landing
concept: For take-off and landing with a single lever, advance the lever to the take-off power position as specified in the POH. The system automatically sets the correct RPM and MAP combination. For approach and landing, set the lever to the approach power position. Full forward for go-around gives maximum power.
exam_tip: Single lever: advance to take-off position for max power. The system manages RPM and MAP automatically.
key_facts: Advance to take-off position for full take-off power
System automatically optimises RPM and MAP
Full forward for go-around gives maximum available power
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal handling of the power control for a single lever control system: (d) when increasing or decreasing power in the air
concept: When increasing or decreasing power with a single lever, simply move the lever forward or back as needed. The system prevents overboosting automatically. There's no need to sequence RPM and MAP separately as with a two-lever system. Just move the lever smoothly and check that engine instruments respond normally.
exam_tip: Single lever: just move the lever -- the system prevents overboosting. No need to sequence RPM and MAP.
key_facts: Smoothly advance or retard the single power lever
System automatically prevents overboosting
No need to manage RPM/MAP sequencing
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the types of failure which can affect the CSU.
concept: CSU failures fall into a few categories. Hydraulic system failures are common—if the engine-driven pump fails or oil pressure drops, the governor can't change pitch. The prop usually defaults to a safe pitch (usually coarse) via a spring return. If you lose oil completely, you might hear a bang and the prop will pitch fully. Control linkage failures can jam the prop lever or prevent pitch changes; this is usually discovered during the CSU exercise check. Governor failures include stuck weights, broken springs, or valve seizing—the governor might not sense RPM correctly or might not modulate pitch. If the governor's stuck, RPM will hunt wildly or the prop won't pitch. Bearing wear or blade erosion causes imbalance, vibration, and eventual fatigue failure. Corrosion in the propeller hub or blade (especially in salt-air environments around the NZ coast) can cause stress concentration and cracking. Ice accretion on the blade leading edge shifts the centre of pressure and changes RPM unpredictably—this is why modern aircraft have prop ice protection. If anything feels wrong during exercise (no RPM response, excessive vibration, grinding sounds), don't fly the aircraft.
exam_tip: Common failures: pump failure, governor failure, control linkage jam. Always exercise the CSU before flight. Any anomaly = don't fly.
key_facts: Hydraulic system failure (pump or pressure loss) prevents pitch change
Spring defaults prop to safe pitch if hydraulic pressure is lost
Control linkage failure prevents pilot from moving prop lever
Governor failure (stuck weights, broken spring) prevents RPM regulation
Bearing wear and blade erosion cause imbalance and vibration
Corrosion in hub or blade creates fatigue stress points
Ice accretion on blade leading edge shifts centre of pressure
CSU exercise check during runup detects most failures
Abnormal vibration or no RPM response during exercise = no-fly condition
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, propeller maintenance and troubleshooting

syllabus_desc: Explain the: (a) conditions which can lead to a 'runaway propeller'
concept: A runaway propeller occurs when the governor fails to control blade pitch and the propeller moves toward fine pitch uncontrollably, causing RPM to increase rapidly beyond normal limits. Causes include governor malfunction, loss of governor oil pressure, or a broken counterweight mechanism. The propeller overspeeds, creating enormous stress on the engine and propeller.
exam_tip: Runaway prop: uncontrolled fine pitch = rapid RPM increase. Caused by governor failure or oil pressure loss.
key_facts: Propeller moves uncontrollably toward fine pitch
RPM increases rapidly beyond normal limits
Causes: governor malfunction, oil pressure loss, mechanical failure
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the: (b) required remedial action to manage a 'runaway propeller'
concept: If you experience a runaway prop: immediately reduce throttle to minimum, reduce airspeed (pull up if safe to do so), and prop lever to full coarse. If RPM doesn't reduce, shut down the engine to prevent catastrophic failure. Feather if able. Land as soon as practicable. Continued operation at overspeed risks engine seizure or propeller separation.
exam_tip: Runaway prop: throttle closed, airspeed reduce, prop full coarse. If no response, shut down the engine.
key_facts: Immediately reduce throttle to minimum power
Move prop lever to full coarse; reduce airspeed
If RPM doesn't reduce, shut down the engine and feather
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a: (a) manual feather propeller
concept: A manual feather propeller can be rotated past the coarse pitch stop to the feather position (blade approximately 90 degrees to the plane of rotation) by the pilot pulling the prop lever to the feather gate. In feather, the blades are edge-on to the airflow, producing minimal drag. This is used after an engine failure on a multi-engine aircraft to reduce drag from the dead engine.
exam_tip: Feathering puts blades edge-on to airflow (about 90 degrees) -- minimises drag from a failed engine.
key_facts: Blades rotated to approximately 90 degrees (edge-on to airflow)
Minimises drag from a windmilling propeller after engine failure
Pilot manually selects feather via the prop lever
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a: (b) auto-feather propeller
concept: Auto-feather automatically feathers the propeller when engine power drops below a preset threshold. It uses torque sensors or MAP sensors to detect the power loss. Auto-feather activates during take-off and approach (when it's armed) so that if an engine fails during these critical phases, the propeller feathers without pilot intervention, reducing drag immediately.
exam_tip: Auto-feather: automatically feathers when power drops -- armed during take-off and approach for immediate response.
key_facts: Automatically feathers propeller on engine power loss
Uses torque or MAP sensors to detect failure
Armed during critical phases (take-off and approach)
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a: (c) auto-coarsen propeller
concept: An auto-coarsen system automatically moves the propeller to a coarse (but not feathered) pitch setting when the engine is shut down or power drops significantly. This prevents the propeller from windmilling in fine pitch (which creates high drag and may damage the engine). It's simpler than full auto-feather and is used on some single-engine aircraft.
exam_tip: Auto-coarsen moves the prop to coarse pitch (not feather) when power drops -- reduces windmilling drag.
key_facts: Moves propeller to coarse pitch automatically on power loss
Prevents high-drag windmilling in fine pitch
Simpler than auto-feather; used on some single-engine aircraft
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the method of operation of the primary control surfaces found in a light twin aeroplane.
concept: Light twins use mechanical flight controls: control columns/yokes and pedals connected via cables and pushrods to the control surfaces. Ailerons on the wings are operated by rotating the yoke—turn left, left aileron goes up and right goes down, rolling the aircraft. Elevators on the tailplane are operated by pushing and pulling the yoke—push forward pitches nose down, pull back pitches nose up. The rudder on the vertical fin is operated by the pedals—push left foot forward, rudder deflects left, nose yaws left. Cable runs from the cockpit are typically routed along the fuselage with pulleys and fairleads, then connect to pushrods or bellcranks at the control surfaces. Most light twins have cable-and-pulley systems with a total of 2-3 cable runs per control surface for redundancy. Friction is minimised with sealed pulleys and greased cable. A cable tension adjuster maintains proper friction—tight enough to prevent flutter, loose enough to move freely. Some modern twins use pushrod systems (more direct) or hydraulic actuators (on glass cockpits), but pure cable remains reliable and simple.
exam_tip: Cables from cockpit to control surfaces. Ailerons roll, elevators pitch, rudder yaws. Redundant cables for safety. Tension adjusted for friction control.
key_facts: Control column/yoke operates ailerons and elevators
Rudder pedals operate rudder
Primary control inputs transmitted via control cables
Ailerons connected to wing trailing edge for lateral control
Elevators connected to tailplane trailing edge for pitch control
Rudder connected to vertical fin for yaw control
Pulleys and fairleads route cables along fuselage
Redundant cable runs provide safety margin
Cable tension adjuster maintains friction and prevents flutter
Hydraulic or pushrod systems used on some modern aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Piper Aztec control systems

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of a basic FD-AFCS system.
concept: A basic Flight Director and Autopilot Control System (FD-AFCS) is a pilot's assistant in the cockpit. The flight director shows you (on the attitude indicator) a series of crosshairs that command the pitch and roll attitudes needed to fly a given profile—climb, level, descent, or approach. The autopilot is an automatic system that can follow those same commands without pilot input, flying the aircraft hands-off. Together they form an integrated system. The FD helps you visualise what the autopilot will do; you can follow the FD manually or engage the autopilot to fly it automatically. The system needs inputs: altitude selector (where you want to go), heading selector (what direction), and mode selector (climb, level cruise, descent, approach). The autopilot then controls the elevators and ailerons (through servo motors or actuators) to maintain those targets. You still manage power, engine parameters, and make strategic decisions—the system flies the profile you've set.
exam_tip: FD shows commanded attitude on the ADI. Autopilot flies those commands automatically. System needs altitude, heading, and mode selection to work.
key_facts: Flight director displays commanded pitch and roll on attitude indicator
Autopilot automatically flies the commanded profile
System integrates navigation data (altitude, heading) with control outputs
Pilot selects mode (climb, level, descent, approach)
Autopilot controls elevators and ailerons via servos or actuators
Pilot retains control of throttle and engine management
System is mode-dependent and can be disengaged by pilot at any time
Reduces workload in cruise and approach phases
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Garmin/Bendix autopilot system manuals

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a basic FD-AFCS system.
concept: Operation's straightforward once you understand the workflow. You engage the system (usually a master switch), select your desired mode (climb, level cruise, descent, approach), set your altitude target and heading target using the panel selectors. The autopilot computes the pitch and roll needed to achieve those targets. It sends servo commands to the elevator and aileron actuators (usually hydraulic or electric). Those actuators move the control surfaces smoothly. Feedback sensors (called gyros or accelerometers, depending on system age) continuously measure actual pitch, roll, and heading, and the autopilot compares actual to commanded and adjusts servo output to correct any deviation. The flight director presents this same commanded attitude on the attitude indicator as a magenta or green crosshair symbol—if you're flying manually, you follow the crosshair. If the autopilot's engaged, it's automatically flying the crosshairs. The system constantly hunts for equilibrium: if you're too high, it pitches nose down; too far left, it rolls right. All this happens many times per second, smoothing the aircraft's flight and reducing pilot workload.
exam_tip: Select mode and targets. System computes needed attitudes. Servos move surfaces. Feedback sensors maintain control. FD shows commanded attitude.
key_facts: System requires mode selection (climb, level, descent, approach)
Altitude and heading targets set via panel selectors
Autopilot computes pitch and roll needed to meet targets
Servo actuators physically move elevators and ailerons
Feedback sensors (gyros/accelerometers) measure actual attitude
Continuous comparison of actual vs. commanded attitude
Servo output continuously adjusted to minimise deviation
Flight director displays commanded attitude on ADI for manual following
Control loops run continuously to maintain smooth, stable flight
Pilot can disengage autopilot and resume manual control at any time
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bendix/Garmin system operation manuals

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical: (a) vertical operational modes
concept: Vertical operational modes of a flight director / autopilot control pitch to maintain a vertical profile. Common modes include: altitude hold (maintains a selected altitude), vertical speed (maintains a selected rate of climb or descent), IAS hold (adjusts pitch to maintain a selected airspeed), and glideslope (tracks an ILS glideslope signal). The pilot selects the desired mode and the system commands the pitch inputs.
exam_tip: Vertical modes control pitch: altitude hold, VS, IAS hold, and glideslope are the common ones.
key_facts: Altitude hold: maintains selected altitude
Vertical speed: maintains selected rate of climb/descent
IAS hold: adjusts pitch to maintain airspeed; Glideslope: tracks ILS GS
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical: (b) lateral operational modes
concept: Lateral operational modes control roll to maintain a lateral track or heading. Common modes include: heading hold (maintains a selected heading), nav mode (tracks a VOR radial or GPS course), approach mode (tracks an ILS localiser), and wing leveller (simply keeps wings level). The pilot selects the mode and the system commands the roll inputs to track the selected course.
exam_tip: Lateral modes control roll: heading, nav tracking, localiser, and wing leveller are the common ones.
key_facts: Heading mode: maintains selected heading
Nav mode: tracks VOR radial or GPS course
Approach/localiser: tracks ILS localiser for precision approaches
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With regard to the operation of a FD-AFCS system, explain the: (a) limitations
concept: FD-AFCS (flight director/autopilot flight control system) limitations include maximum and minimum operating speeds, maximum turbulence intensity, altitude limits, and minimum engagement altitude. The autopilot cannot fly the aircraft in all conditions -- it has servo torque limits and can be overwhelmed by severe turbulence or icing. Know the limitations in your aircraft's POH.
exam_tip: Autopilot has speed, altitude, and turbulence limits -- it can be overwhelmed. Know your POH limitations.
key_facts: Maximum and minimum operating speed limits
Not designed for severe turbulence or icing conditions
Servo torque limits can be exceeded in extreme conditions
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With regard to the operation of a FD-AFCS system, explain the: (b) cautions
concept: FD-AFCS cautions include: always monitor the autopilot (it can malfunction or fly an incorrect profile), be ready to disconnect immediately if it behaves unexpectedly, don't over-rely on automation at the expense of basic flying skills, and ensure the correct mode is engaged before looking away. An autopilot can fly you into terrain, obstacles, or an unusual attitude if improperly managed.
exam_tip: Always monitor the autopilot -- it can malfunction. Be ready to disconnect instantly and hand-fly.
key_facts: Never set and forget -- actively monitor autopilot behaviour
Be ready to disconnect immediately if behaviour is unexpected
Incorrect mode selection can lead to dangerous flight paths
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the: (a) function of a cockpit fitted or portable oxygen system
concept: A cockpit oxygen system provides supplemental oxygen to the crew and passengers at altitudes where the reduced atmospheric pressure makes normal breathing insufficient. Above 10,000 ft, the partial pressure of oxygen decreases enough to impair mental function (hypoxia). In NZ, supplemental oxygen is required above 10,000 ft cabin altitude for extended periods.
exam_tip: Oxygen systems combat hypoxia at altitude. In NZ, supplemental oxygen required above 10,000 ft cabin altitude.
key_facts: Provides supplemental oxygen to prevent hypoxia at altitude
Required above 10,000 ft cabin altitude for extended flight
Hypoxia impairs judgement and reaction time before the pilot notices symptoms
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the: (b) principle of operation of a cockpit fitted or portable oxygen system
concept: Cockpit oxygen systems store oxygen as compressed gas in a cylinder or as chemical oxygen (in portable systems). The oxygen flows through a regulator that controls the flow rate and pressure, then to a face mask worn by the user. Diluter-demand systems mix oxygen with cabin air and increase the oxygen percentage with altitude. Continuous-flow systems deliver a steady stream through a cannula or mask.
exam_tip: Two main types: diluter-demand (mixes O2 with air, adjusts with altitude) and continuous-flow (steady stream).
key_facts: Compressed gas cylinder or chemical oxygen source
Regulator controls flow rate and delivery pressure
Diluter-demand adjusts O2 percentage; continuous-flow provides steady stream
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (a) arm (moment arm)
concept: The arm (moment arm) is the horizontal distance from the datum to any point on the aircraft where a weight is located, measured in inches or millimetres. Arms aft of the datum are positive; arms forward of the datum are negative. Every item loaded has an arm that determines its effect on the aircraft's centre of gravity.
exam_tip: Arm = horizontal distance from datum to a weight's location. Positive aft, negative forward of datum.
key_facts: Horizontal distance from the datum to a weight location
Measured in inches or millimetres
Arms aft of datum are positive; forward are negative
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (b) datum
concept: The datum is an imaginary vertical reference plane from which all horizontal measurements (arms) are taken. It's chosen by the manufacturer and specified in the aircraft's weight and balance documentation. The datum might be the firewall, the nose, the leading edge of the wing, or even a point ahead of the aircraft. All arms are measured from this single reference.
exam_tip: The datum is the fixed reference point for all arm measurements -- it's defined by the manufacturer, not the pilot.
key_facts: Imaginary vertical reference plane for measuring all arms
Defined by the manufacturer in the weight and balance data
Common locations: firewall, wing leading edge, or ahead of the nose
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (c) moment (including units used)
concept: A moment is the turning force created by a weight at a given distance from the datum. Moment = weight x arm, measured in pound-inches (lb-in) or kilogram-millimetres (kg-mm). Moments are the key to all weight and balance calculations -- the total moment divided by the total weight gives the centre of gravity position.
exam_tip: Moment = weight x arm. Total moment / total weight = C of G position.
key_facts: Moment = weight x arm
Measured in lb-in, lb-ft, or kg-mm
Total moment / total weight = centre of gravity location
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (d) centre of gravity (C of G)
concept: The centre of gravity (C of G) is the single point where the aircraft's total weight is considered to act. It's the balance point -- if you could support the aircraft at just that one point, it would balance perfectly. The C of G position is critical for stability and control: it must stay within specified limits for safe flight.
exam_tip: C of G = the balance point where total weight acts. Must be within limits for safe flight.
key_facts: The point where total weight is considered to act
Calculated as total moment / total weight
Must remain within manufacturer-specified limits for safe flight
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (e) longitudinal C of G range and limits
concept: The longitudinal C of G range defines the forward and aft limits within which the C of G must fall for safe flight. These limits are determined during certification and published in the POH/loading documentation. Loading the aircraft with the C of G outside these limits creates dangerous handling characteristics.
exam_tip: C of G must stay within forward and aft limits -- outside these limits, the aircraft may be uncontrollable.
key_facts: Defined by forward limit and aft limit
Published in the POH and weight and balance documentation
C of G outside limits creates dangerous stability and control issues
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (f) station
concept: A station is a specific location along the longitudinal axis of the aircraft, identified by its arm (distance from the datum). Station numbers are used to identify seat positions, baggage compartments, and fuel tank locations in weight and balance calculations. Each station has a defined arm that's used to calculate moments.
exam_tip: Stations are specific loading points identified by their arm distance from the datum.
key_facts: Specific locations along the aircraft identified by their arm
Used to locate seats, baggage compartments, and fuel tanks
Each station's arm is used in moment calculations
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (g) index units
concept: Index units are a simplified method of expressing moments to make weight and balance calculations easier. Instead of working with large moment numbers, the moment is divided by a reduction factor to give a smaller index number. The loading graph converts weight at each station directly to index units, and the C of G envelope uses index units on one axis.
exam_tip: Index units simplify moment calculations by dividing moments by a constant -- read directly from loading graphs.
key_facts: Simplified moments: actual moment / reduction factor = index units
Makes weight and balance arithmetic simpler
Read directly from loading graphs in the POH
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (h) basic empty weight (empty aircraft weight)
concept: Basic empty weight (BEW) is the weight of the aircraft as it left the factory, including the airframe, engine, fixed equipment, unusable fuel, and undrainable oil. It does not include usable fuel, crew, passengers, or baggage. The BEW and its associated C of G position are recorded in the aircraft's weight schedule.
exam_tip: BEW includes airframe, engine, fixed equipment, unusable fuel, and undrainable oil -- nothing else.
key_facts: Airframe + engine + fixed equipment + unusable fuel + undrainable oil
Does not include usable fuel, oil (usable), crew, or payload
Recorded in the aircraft's weight schedule with its C of G
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (i) empty weight centre of gravity position
concept: The empty weight C of G position is the balance point of the aircraft in its basic empty weight condition. It's established during the last weighing of the aircraft and recorded in the weight schedule. Any modifications (added equipment, paint) change both the BEW and its C of G position, requiring an update to the weight schedule.
exam_tip: Empty weight C of G comes from the last aircraft weighing -- modifications require re-weighing.
key_facts: Balance point of the aircraft at basic empty weight
Established by physical weighing of the aircraft
Changes with any modification -- weight schedule must be updated
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (j) basic operating weight (aircraft prepared for service weight)
concept: Basic operating weight (BOW), also called 'aircraft prepared for service weight', is the basic empty weight plus standard items needed for flight -- the crew, crew baggage, catering, and usable oil. It's the weight of the aircraft ready to go but with no passengers, cargo, or usable fuel loaded.
exam_tip: BOW = BEW + crew + crew baggage + usable oil. It's the aircraft ready for service, minus fuel and payload.
key_facts: BEW + crew + crew baggage + usable oil
Aircraft ready for service without passengers or usable fuel
Starting point for calculating payload and fuel loading
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (k) payload (commercial load)
concept: Payload (commercial load) is the revenue-earning or mission-essential weight carried on the flight -- passengers, baggage, and cargo. It's the difference between the zero fuel weight and the operating weight. Payload is what makes the flight commercially worthwhile, but it must be balanced against fuel requirements.
exam_tip: Payload = passengers + baggage + cargo. It's what earns the revenue but competes with fuel for available weight.
key_facts: Passengers + baggage + cargo (revenue-generating load)
Payload = ZFW - BOW
Must be balanced against fuel requirements within maximum weight limits
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (l) zero fuel weight (ZFW)
concept: Zero fuel weight (ZFW) is the total weight of the aircraft loaded with everything except usable fuel. ZFW = BOW + payload. Some aircraft have a maximum ZFW limit to prevent excessive wing root bending loads -- fuel in the wings partially counteracts wing bending by acting as a relief load.
exam_tip: ZFW = BOW + payload (everything except usable fuel). Max ZFW limits protect wing root structure.
key_facts: ZFW = BOW + payload (all loaded weight except usable fuel)
Some aircraft have a maximum ZFW structural limit
Wing fuel provides bending relief -- too little fuel with heavy payload stresses the wing root
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (m) ramp weight
concept: Ramp weight is the total weight of the aircraft fully loaded and ready to taxi, including fuel that will be consumed during taxi and run-up. It's slightly higher than take-off weight because some fuel is burned before reaching the runway. Ramp weight must not exceed the maximum ramp weight specified in the POH.
exam_tip: Ramp weight = take-off weight + taxi fuel. It's the heaviest the aircraft will be.
key_facts: Total weight of the fully loaded aircraft ready to taxi
Includes taxi and run-up fuel
Ramp weight = take-off weight + taxi fuel burned before take-off
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (n) gross weight (AUW)
concept: Gross weight (all-up weight, AUW) is the total weight of the aircraft at any given moment during the flight. It decreases as fuel is burned. Gross weight must not exceed the relevant maximum weight limit (take-off, landing, or zero fuel) at the appropriate phase of flight.
exam_tip: Gross weight is the aircraft's total weight at any given moment -- it decreases as fuel burns off.
key_facts: Total aircraft weight at any point during the flight
Decreases as fuel is consumed
Must not exceed the applicable maximum weight for each flight phase
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (o) take-off weight
concept: Take-off weight is the gross weight of the aircraft at the start of the take-off roll. It equals ramp weight minus taxi fuel. Take-off weight must not exceed the maximum certificated take-off weight (MCTOW) or the performance-limited take-off weight for the conditions.
exam_tip: Take-off weight = ramp weight - taxi fuel. Must not exceed MCTOW or performance-limited weight.
key_facts: Gross weight at the start of the take-off roll
Take-off weight = ramp weight - taxi fuel
Must not exceed MCTOW or performance-limited take-off weight
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (p) maximum certificated take-off weight (MCTOW)
concept: Maximum certificated take-off weight (MCTOW) is the highest weight at which the aircraft is certified to commence a take-off. It's a structural limit determined during certification and never changes regardless of conditions. The actual allowable take-off weight may be less than MCTOW due to performance limitations (runway length, temperature, altitude).
exam_tip: MCTOW is a fixed structural limit -- actual allowable take-off weight may be lower due to performance constraints.
key_facts: Maximum weight certified for take-off -- a structural limit
Fixed by certification, does not change with conditions
Actual take-off weight may be limited below MCTOW by performance factors
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (q) landing weight
concept: Landing weight is the gross weight of the aircraft at the moment of touchdown. It's lower than take-off weight by the amount of fuel burned during the flight. Landing weight must not exceed the maximum certificated landing weight (MCLW).
exam_tip: Landing weight = take-off weight - fuel burned in flight. Must not exceed MCLW.
key_facts: Gross weight at the moment of touchdown
Landing weight = take-off weight - trip fuel
Must not exceed MCLW
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (r) maximum certificated landing weight (MCLW)
concept: Maximum certificated landing weight (MCLW) is the highest weight at which the aircraft is certified to land. It's a structural limit protecting the landing gear and airframe from excessive landing loads. MCLW is usually lower than MCTOW because landing loads are higher than take-off loads. If MCLW would be exceeded, the aircraft must hold or dump fuel before landing.
exam_tip: MCLW is usually lower than MCTOW. If overweight for landing, hold to burn fuel or dump fuel (if equipped).
key_facts: Maximum weight certified for landing -- structural limit
Usually lower than MCTOW due to higher landing loads
If overweight, hold to burn fuel or dump fuel before landing
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (s) centre of gravity envelope
concept: The C of G envelope is a graphical chart that shows the allowable weight and C of G combinations for the aircraft. The x-axis shows C of G position (arm) and the y-axis shows weight. The aircraft's loaded weight and C of G must plot inside the envelope boundary at take-off, during flight, and at landing to ensure safe handling.
exam_tip: The C of G envelope shows allowable weight/CG combinations -- plot your point and confirm it falls inside the boundary.
key_facts: Graphical representation of allowable weight and C of G limits
Weight on one axis, C of G position on the other
Aircraft must be within envelope at take-off, during flight, and at landing
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (t) specific gravity
concept: Specific gravity (SG) is the ratio of a substance's density to the density of water. Water has an SG of 1.0. AVGAS has an SG of approximately 0.72 (lighter than water), meaning one litre of AVGAS weighs about 0.72 kg. Jet A-1 has an SG of approximately 0.80. SG is essential for converting between fuel volume and fuel weight.
exam_tip: SG = density relative to water. AVGAS: SG 0.72 (1 litre = 0.72 kg). Jet A-1: SG 0.80 (1 litre = 0.80 kg).
key_facts: Ratio of a substance's density to the density of water
AVGAS 100LL: SG approximately 0.72
Jet A-1: SG approximately 0.80
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (u) the specific gravity and the weight of fuel of AVGAS or Jet A-1
concept: AVGAS 100LL has a specific gravity of approximately 0.72, so one litre weighs about 0.72 kg (or one US gallon weighs about 6 lb). Jet A-1 has a specific gravity of approximately 0.80, so one litre weighs about 0.80 kg (or one US gallon weighs about 6.7 lb). These values are essential for converting fuel volume (litres or gallons) to weight (kg or lb) for weight and balance calculations.
exam_tip: AVGAS: 0.72 kg/litre or 6 lb/US gal. Jet A-1: 0.80 kg/litre or 6.7 lb/US gal. Critical for W&B calculations.
key_facts: AVGAS 100LL: ~0.72 kg/litre or ~6 lb/US gallon
Jet A-1: ~0.80 kg/litre or ~6.7 lb/US gallon
SG varies slightly with temperature -- these are standard approximations
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the principles of aeroplane balance.
concept: An aeroplane balances when the total moment (weight times arm) from the nose to the C of G equals the total moment from the C of G to the tail. The C of G is the fulcrum. Imagine a seesaw: if you put a heavy weight close to the pivot and a lighter weight far away, they can balance. Same with an aircraft. If you put 1000 lbs of cargo in the aft fuselage (high arm), it creates more moment than 500 lbs of cargo in a forward compartment (low arm), even though it weighs more. The fuselage is like that seesaw—the aircraft balances (doesn't pitch) when forward moments equal aft moments. However, the aircraft is dynamic (it's flying), so static balance isn't enough. The wings must generate lift forward of the C of G (that's part of how the design works—wings are usually mounted slightly forward). The tail must generate a small downforce aft of the C of G to balance the moment created by the wing's forward lift. If the C of G moves too far forward, the tail can't generate enough downforce to balance (you get a pitch-down tendency, very nose-heavy). If the C of G moves too far aft, the tail would need to generate upward force (lift) to balance, which reduces the net lifting capacity of the aircraft and makes it unstable. That's why we have C of G limits—to ensure the tail can balance the wing and maintain stable, controllable flight.
exam_tip: Balance = moments balanced around C of G. Static balance (seesaw) must be checked. But the tail also needs to balance the wing's moment.
key_facts: Balance occurs when forward moments equal aft moments
C of G is the balance point
Larger arm × smaller weight = same moment as smaller arm × larger weight
Forward C of G limit: tail can't generate enough downforce to balance wing
Aft C of G limit: tail generates downforce, reducing net lift and causing instability
Stability requires C of G forward of neutral point (where wing creates lift)
Tail provides balancing downforce to maintain stability
Load distribution affects C of G position
Fuel burn during flight shifts C of G (fuel tanks distributed forward and aft)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, weight and balance principles

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the tailplane in providing the final longitudinal balancing force.
concept: The tailplane (horizontal stabiliser) is your aircraft's trim tab for the whole fuselage. The wings generate lift ahead of the C of G; that forward lift creates a nose-up moment. If the tail did nothing, the aircraft would pitch nose-up constantly. So the tailplane generates a small downforce (negative lift) aft of the C of G, creating a nose-down moment that exactly balances the nose-up moment from the wings. The result: the aircraft hangs in balance and flies level. This downforce is active—the tailplane must constantly generate it as long as you're flying. If the C of G is very far forward, you need more tailplane downforce to balance; the tail works harder, and you lose some of that downforce as useful lift (waste of potential). If the C of G is too far aft, the tail can't generate enough downforce, or you'd need to use up-elevator (tail generates upforce), which reduces stability and control. The tailplane's size and position are designed with a specific C of G range in mind. Load the aircraft within that range and the tail balances perfectly. Step outside and you either make the tail work too hard (forward C of G) or can't balance at all (aft C of G). That's why we have limits.
exam_tip: Tail generates downforce to balance wing's nose-up moment. Helps define safe C of G range. Too far forward = tail works too hard. Too far aft = can't balance.
key_facts: Tailplane generates downforce to balance wing's nose-up moment
Downforce is created by up-elevator deflection
Forward C of G requires more tail downforce (less efficient)
Aft C of G requires less tail downforce (or upforce—less stable)
Forward C of G limit: beyond this, tail can't balance
Aft C of G limit: beyond this, aircraft becomes unstable and uncontrollable
Tailplane size and design depend on intended C of G range
Downforce is continuous—the tail is always working to maintain balance
Load distribution and fuel burn affect how much tail force is needed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, longitudinal stability and balance

syllabus_desc: Explain the significance of lateral fuel imbalance and the limitations typically applied.
concept: Fuel's stored in the wings, and if you have different amounts in the left and right tanks, the aircraft's laterally imbalanced. More fuel on one side = heavier on that side = that wing wants to drop. You compensate by holding aileron (left aileron down to lift the heavier side, or right aileron up—both do the same thing). That's work, and it degrades performance (you're constantly correcting instead of flying clean). In a twin-engine aircraft, if the lateral imbalance is severe, you might get control issues. The usual limit is something like 10% of total usable fuel in the tanks (e.g., 200 lbs difference if you've got 2000 lbs of usable fuel total). So you cross-feed occasionally during flight to balance, or you fill the tanks equally before flight. After landing, if you've burned more from one side than the other, you'll note the imbalance and factor it into your fuel planning for the next flight. Some aircraft have fuel-selector valves that let you cross-feed (pump fuel from one side to the other) in flight. Others have no cross-feed capability, so you're stuck with whatever imbalance develops due to unequal burn rates. The imbalance gradually resolves as you burn fuel equally from both sides (though engines rarely burn exactly equally).
exam_tip: Lateral imbalance limits usually around 10% of total usable fuel. Cross-feed to balance. Affects control input and performance.
key_facts: Lateral imbalance created by unequal fuel in left and right tanks
Heavier wing wants to drop; requires constant aileron correction
Correction degrades performance and increases pilot workload
Typical limit: 10% of total usable fuel difference
Cross-feed capability (if available) allows balancing fuel in flight
Unequal engine fuel consumption creates imbalance over time
Imbalance affects aircraft stability and control
Post-flight: note imbalance for next flight planning
Symmetrical loading is ideal for performance and control
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, weight and balance limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on longitudinal stability and resultant handling issues with the aeroplane loaded with the centre of gravity: (a) forward
concept: With the C of G forward (within limits), the aircraft is more stable but less manoeuvrable. The tail must produce a larger down-force to balance the forward weight, which increases total drag and fuel consumption. Higher stick forces are needed to pitch up, and stall speed increases slightly. Forward C of G is the safer condition for stability.
exam_tip: Forward C of G: more stable, higher stall speed, more drag, higher fuel consumption, heavier stick forces.
key_facts: More longitudinally stable -- harder to stall
Larger tail down-force required -- increases drag and fuel burn
Higher stick forces and slightly higher stall speed
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on longitudinal stability and resultant handling issues with the aeroplane loaded with the centre of gravity: (b) outside the forward limit
concept: With the C of G forward of the forward limit, the tail may not produce enough down-force to flare for landing, or the elevator may reach full up-deflection before adequate nose-up pitch is achieved. The aircraft may be unable to rotate for take-off or flare for landing at low speeds. This is a dangerous condition.
exam_tip: C of G ahead of forward limit: may not be able to flare for landing or rotate for take-off. Extremely dangerous.
key_facts: Elevator may reach full deflection before adequate pitch change
May not be able to rotate for take-off or flare for landing
Dangerously high stall speed and extreme stick forces
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on longitudinal stability and resultant handling issues with the aeroplane loaded with the centre of gravity: (c) aft
concept: With the C of G aft (within limits), the aircraft is less stable but more manoeuvrable. Lighter stick forces, lower stall speed, and less trim drag (the tail produces less down-force). Cruise performance may improve slightly. However, the aircraft is more sensitive to pitch inputs and requires more pilot attention.
exam_tip: Aft C of G: lighter stick forces, lower stall speed, less drag -- but reduced stability needs more pilot attention.
key_facts: Less stable, more manoeuvrable, lighter stick forces
Lower stall speed and less trim drag (improved cruise efficiency)
More pitch-sensitive -- requires careful handling
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on longitudinal stability and resultant handling issues with the aeroplane loaded with the centre of gravity: (d) outside the aft limit
concept: With the C of G aft of the aft limit, the aircraft becomes unstable -- it won't naturally return to trimmed flight after a disturbance. Any pitch displacement gets worse instead of correcting. The aircraft may be impossible to control, especially at low speeds. Stall recovery may be impossible because the elevator can't produce enough nose-down moment. This is the most dangerous C of G condition.
exam_tip: C of G behind the aft limit: unstable, may be impossible to control. Stall recovery can be impossible. Most dangerous condition.
key_facts: Aircraft becomes longitudinally unstable -- divergent pitch response
May be impossible to recover from a stall or unusual attitude
Most dangerous C of G condition -- never load beyond the aft limit
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the expected effect of fuel burn on the centre of gravity position.
concept: Fuel in the wings is forward of the C of G for most aircraft (the main-tank sips are typically at about 40% of the wing chord, well ahead of where the C of G ends up). As you burn fuel, you're removing weight from ahead of the C of G, so the C of G moves aft. This is expected and predicted—your weight-and-balance calculation at takeoff anticipates this. If you've planned correctly, the C of G moves from a forward position at takeoff and gradually drifts aft as fuel burns, ending up near the aft limit at landing. That's the designed flight envelope. Some aircraft have aft-fuselage fuel tanks (reserve tanks, auxiliary tanks); those are further aft, and burning them moves the C of G forward. If you've got centre-section tanks (in the wing root or centre of the fuselage), burning those has minimal C of G effect because they're near the C of G already. Most light twins are designed with main tanks well forward, so normal fuel burn always moves the C of G aft. You track this by calculating C of G at various weights during flight planning: at takeoff (heavy, C of G forward), at top of climb (some fuel burned, C of G slightly aft), at landing (most fuel burned, C of G furthest aft). As long as you stay within the envelope, you're fine.
exam_tip: Fuel burn moves C of G aft (in typical aircraft where wing tanks are forward). Plan for this in weight and balance. Final C of G at landing should be within limits.
key_facts: Fuel stored in wings is typically ahead of the C of G
Burning fuel (weight removal from forward) moves C of G aft
Expected and predicted in weight-and-balance calculations
Takeoff: C of G is forward (heavy with fuel)
Cruise: C of G drifts aft as fuel burns
Landing: C of G is further aft than takeoff (lightest condition)
Final C of G must remain within aft limit
Aft-fuselage fuel tanks (if any) move C of G forward when burned
Wing tanks have greater C of G effect than fuselage tanks
Flight plan should verify C of G remains in envelope throughout flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, fuel burn and C of G shift

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate conversion data, convert between: (a) US gallons and litres
concept: To convert between US gallons and litres: 1 US gallon = 3.785 litres. To go from gallons to litres, multiply by 3.785. To go from litres to gallons, divide by 3.785. Note: US gallons are different from Imperial gallons (1 Imperial gallon = 4.546 litres). Aircraft fuel in NZ is typically measured in litres but US-manufactured aircraft POHs may use US gallons.
exam_tip: 1 US gallon = 3.785 litres. Watch for the US vs Imperial gallon trap -- they're different!
key_facts: 1 US gallon = 3.785 litres
1 Imperial gallon = 4.546 litres (different from US gallon)
US gallons x 3.785 = litres; litres / 3.785 = US gallons
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate conversion data, convert between: (b) pounds and kilograms
concept: To convert between pounds (lb) and kilograms (kg): 1 kg = 2.205 lb, or 1 lb = 0.4536 kg. Multiply kg by 2.205 to get pounds; multiply pounds by 0.4536 to get kg. These conversions are essential for weight and balance when the POH uses different units from your loading data.
exam_tip: 1 kg = 2.205 lb. Quick check: kg x 2.2 = lb; lb / 2.2 = kg.
key_facts: Multiply kg by 2.205 to get pounds
Multiply pounds by 0.4536 to get kilograms
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given the fuel's specific gravity, calculate the weight of a given volume of fuel.
concept: Specific gravity is the density of a substance compared to water. Water weighs 8.345 lbs per US gallon (or 1000 kg per cubic metre). AVGAS has a specific gravity of about 0.72, meaning it's 72% as dense as water. So one gallon of AVGAS weighs 0.72 × 8.345 = 6.01 lbs (roughly 6 lbs). Jet A-1 has a specific gravity of about 0.80, so one gallon weighs 0.80 × 8.345 = 6.67 lbs (roughly 6.7 lbs). If you've got 100 US gallons of AVGAS, weight = 100 × 6.01 = 601 lbs. If you've got 100 US gallons of Jet A-1, weight = 100 × 6.67 = 667 lbs. In metric, water weighs 1 kg per litre. AVGAS weighs 0.72 kg/litre, Jet A-1 weighs 0.80 kg/litre. So 200 litres of AVGAS = 200 × 0.72 = 144 kg. The AFM/POH always specifies which fuel the aircraft uses and gives you the weight per gallon (or per litre) in the specifications section. But if they give you specific gravity, you can calculate it yourself.
exam_tip: Fuel weight = volume × specific gravity × water density. AVGAS ≈ 6 lbs/gallon or 0.72 kg/litre. Jet A-1 ≈ 6.7 lbs/gallon or 0.80 kg/litre.
key_facts: Specific gravity compares fuel density to water
Water weighs 8.345 lbs per US gallon (or 1 kg per litre)
Fuel weight = volume × specific gravity × water density
AVGAS specific gravity approximately 0.72
AVGAS weighs approximately 6 lbs per US gallon or 0.72 kg per litre
Jet A-1 specific gravity approximately 0.80
Jet A-1 weighs approximately 6.7 lbs per US gallon or 0.80 kg per litre
AFM/POH specifies fuel type and weight per unit volume
Always confirm fuel type before flight planning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, fuel properties and calculations

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate loading data: (a) calculate the take-off weight
concept: Take-off weight = basic empty weight + fuel weight + crew weight + passenger weight + baggage weight. Add up all the individual weights at their respective stations. Ensure the total doesn't exceed MCTOW. Remember to convert fuel volume to weight using the correct specific gravity.
exam_tip: Add up all individual weights: BEW + fuel + crew + passengers + baggage = take-off weight. Check against MCTOW.
key_facts: Sum all loaded weights: BEW + usable fuel + crew + payload
Convert fuel volume to weight using SG (AVGAS: 0.72 kg/L)
Total must not exceed MCTOW
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate loading data: (b) calculate the landing weight
concept: Landing weight = take-off weight minus the fuel burned during the flight (trip fuel). Calculate the expected fuel burn based on flight time and fuel consumption rate. Landing weight must not exceed MCLW.
exam_tip: Landing weight = take-off weight - trip fuel. Check against MCLW.
key_facts: Landing weight = take-off weight - trip fuel burned
Trip fuel = flight time x fuel consumption rate
Must not exceed MCLW
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate loading data: (c) calculate the landing weight for a limited take-off weight flight
concept: For a limited take-off weight flight (where the aircraft can't take off at MCTOW due to performance limitations), calculate the landing weight by subtracting trip fuel from the performance-limited take-off weight. The landing weight may still need to be checked against MCLW.
exam_tip: Performance-limited TOW may be less than MCTOW -- subtract trip fuel to get landing weight, then check MCLW.
key_facts: Performance-limited TOW may be lower than MCTOW
Landing weight = performance-limited TOW - trip fuel
Still check that landing weight doesn't exceed MCLW
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate loading data: (d) for a specific maximum take-off weight, calculate the payload available
concept: Payload available = MCTOW - BOW - fuel weight. To calculate maximum payload for a given take-off weight, subtract the basic operating weight and the required fuel weight from the maximum (or specified) take-off weight. If fuel is fixed, the remaining capacity is available for payload.
exam_tip: Payload available = max TOW - BOW - fuel weight. More fuel means less payload and vice versa.
key_facts: Available payload = max take-off weight - BOW - fuel loaded
Fuel and payload compete for the remaining weight capacity
Always check that the resulting C of G is also within limits
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate loading data: (e) for a specific maximum zero fuel weight, calculate the payload available
concept: For a maximum zero fuel weight limit, payload available = max ZFW - BOW. The ZFW limit is independent of fuel loading. Once the ZFW payload is determined, fuel can be added up to the remaining capacity before reaching MCTOW. The ZFW limit protects the wing root from excessive bending loads.
exam_tip: Max payload from ZFW limit = max ZFW - BOW. Then add fuel up to MCTOW. ZFW limits protect wing root structure.
key_facts: Payload limited by ZFW = max ZFW - BOW
Additional fuel can be loaded up to MCTOW after ZFW payload is loaded
ZFW limit protects wing root from excessive bending without fuel relief
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate loading data: (f) calculate the allowable load in various compartments
concept: Each baggage compartment has a maximum load limit (in kg or lb) published in the POH. This is a structural limit for the compartment floor. Calculate the allowable load by checking: the compartment maximum weight, the remaining weight capacity before exceeding MCTOW, and the C of G effect of loading at that station.
exam_tip: Compartment load limits are structural -- don't exceed them even if the aircraft is under MCTOW.
key_facts: Each compartment has a maximum load limit (structural)
Check compartment limit, MCTOW, and C of G effect
Exceeding compartment limits risks structural damage regardless of total weight
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given appropriate loading data and graphs and using a typical weight and balance work sheet, calculate the longitudinal centre of gravity position: (a) at take-off
concept: To calculate the C of G at take-off: list all items (BEW, fuel, crew, passengers, baggage) with their weights and arms. Calculate each item's moment (weight x arm). Sum all weights and sum all moments. Divide total moment by total weight to get the C of G position. Plot on the C of G envelope to confirm it's within limits at take-off weight.
exam_tip: C of G at take-off = total moment / total weight. Plot on the envelope to verify within limits.
key_facts: List all weights with their respective arms
Calculate moments (weight x arm) for each item
Total moment / total weight = C of G position; verify on envelope
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given appropriate loading data and graphs and using a typical weight and balance work sheet, calculate the longitudinal centre of gravity position: (b) for landing
concept: To calculate the C of G at landing: start with the take-off weight and C of G, then subtract the fuel burned during the flight. The fuel burn reduces the total weight and changes the total moment (subtract the fuel weight x fuel arm). Recalculate: new C of G = new total moment / new total weight. Verify the landing weight and C of G are within the envelope.
exam_tip: Landing C of G: subtract burned fuel's weight and moment from take-off totals. Re-plot on envelope.
key_facts: Subtract trip fuel weight and its moment from take-off totals
New C of G = (take-off moment - fuel moment) / (take-off weight - fuel weight)
Verify both landing weight and C of G are within envelope limits
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Plot the longitudinal and lateral centre of gravity positions on a graph showing the centre of gravity limits.
concept: The C of G graph is a plot with longitudinal position (usually in inches from datum or as a percentage of mean aerodynamic chord) on the horizontal axis and gross weight on the vertical axis. The safe zone is outlined—it's typically a rectangle or polygon. You calculate your C of G position (in inches or %) and your total weight, then locate that point on the graph. If the point falls within the outlined zone, you're good. If it's outside, you're not allowed to fly. For lateral C of G, the graph is similar: lateral position (left to right) on the horizontal axis, total weight on the vertical. The lateral envelope is usually quite narrow (maybe ±0.5% of fuselage width, for asymmetric loading). You calculate lateral C of G by the same method (sum of moments laterally, divided by total weight) and plot it. In practice, unless you're heavily cross-loaded, lateral C of G is rarely a problem—most loading is naturally symmetrical. But it should be checked, especially if you've got an unusual loading (e.g., all cargo on one side). The graph is provided in the AFM/POH. You plot both takeoff and landing C of G positions. If either is outside the envelope, you must replan the loading.
exam_tip: Graph shows safe C of G zone. Plot calculated C of G and weight. Point must fall within zone. If not, replan loading.
key_facts: C of G graph shows safe envelope (zone) for the aircraft
Horizontal axis: C of G position (inches from datum or % MAC)
Vertical axis: gross weight (lbs or kg)
Safe zone: outlined area on graph (rectangle, polygon, or irregular shape)
Calculate C of G and plot the point (weight, C of G position)
Point must fall within the outlined safe zone
Takeoff C of G and landing C of G plotted separately
Lateral C of G graph plotted similarly (left-right position vs. weight)
Lateral envelope usually narrow (±0.5-1% of fuselage width)
If either point is outside envelope, replan loading and recalculate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, C of G graphing procedures

syllabus_desc: Solve the following loading problems: (a) find a revised longitudinal centre of gravity position after loading or offloading weight
concept: To find a revised C of G after loading or offloading weight: calculate the new total weight (old weight +/- change) and new total moment (old moment +/- weight change x its arm). Divide the new total moment by the new total weight to get the revised C of G position. Verify the result is within the C of G envelope.
exam_tip: New C of G = (old moment +/- added/removed moment) / (old weight +/- added/removed weight).
key_facts: New total weight = old weight +/- weight change
New total moment = old moment +/- (weight change x arm)
New C of G = new total moment / new total weight
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Solve the following loading problems: (b) loading or offloading weight to place the longitudinal centre of gravity at a given station
concept: To place the C of G at a given station by loading/offloading weight: this is a reverse calculation. You know the desired C of G and need to find how much weight to add or remove at a specific station. Use: weight change = (total weight x (desired CG - current CG)) / (station arm - desired CG).
exam_tip: Weight to add/remove = total weight x (desired CG - current CG) / (loading arm - desired CG).
key_facts: Rearranges the moment equation to solve for weight change
Weight change = total weight x (desired CG - current CG) / (station arm - desired CG)
Check that the resulting total weight is still within limits
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Solve the following loading problems: (c) loading or offloading weight at a given station without exceeding the longitudinal centre of gravity limits
concept: To load or offload weight at a given station without exceeding C of G limits: calculate the maximum weight that can be added or removed at that station before the C of G reaches the nearest limit (forward or aft). Use the moment equation with the C of G limit as the target and solve for the maximum allowable weight change.
exam_tip: Solve for max weight by substituting the C of G limit into the moment equation -- don't exceed the nearest limit.
key_facts: Calculate maximum weight change before C of G reaches its limit
Use the nearer C of G limit (forward or aft) as the constraint
Also check that total weight remains within MCTOW/MCLW
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Solve the following loading problems: (d) moving weight from one station to another and calculating the revised longitudinal centre of gravity position
concept: When weight is moved from one station to another, total weight stays the same but the C of G shifts. The change in moment = weight moved x (new arm - old arm). The new C of G = old C of G + (weight moved x distance moved) / total weight. This is useful for moving passengers between seats or redistributing baggage.
exam_tip: Moving weight: C of G shift = (weight moved x distance moved) / total weight. Total weight doesn't change.
key_facts: Total weight stays the same -- only C of G changes
Change in moment = weight moved x (new arm - old arm)
New C of G = old C of G + (weight x distance) / total weight
source: Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge
